proposals.title: Spatio-Temporal Graph Filtering for Stability and Efficiency in Large-Scale Consumer IoT Networks
proposals.motivation: The proliferation of consumer IoT devices (smart homes, wearables, connected vehicles) creates large-scale, dynamic networks where local interactions can cascade into system-wide inefficiencies, delays, and instability, mirroring challenges in urban traffic systems. Existing control and anomaly detection methods often lack the scalability and real-time adaptability needed for these heterogeneous, resource-constrained networks. A principled design framework is urgently required to manage these interactions and ensure robust, efficient global behavior.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a distributed spatio-temporal graph filtering framework, inspired by traffic flow modeling and anomaly detection principles, can enforce stability and optimize resource allocation in large-scale consumer IoT networks, preventing congestion and delay propagation.
proposals.method: We will design a hierarchical control architecture where local device clusters act as autonomous agents. A novel Temporal Semantic Transformer (TSTNet) layer will model dynamic inter-cluster dependencies and predict system states. For real-time control, we will develop lightweight, distributed graph filters (extending concepts from UTFP-AD) to process local state information and enforce global constraints through consensus algorithms, preventing anomalous propagation of delays or resource contention. Blockchain-inspired mechanisms will be adapted for secure, decentralized coordination logs.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, scalable control-theoretic framework for consumer IoT system design, directly addressing the 'challenges' of 'real'-time 'delays' and stability. It will provide concrete algorithms for 'vehicle'-like device coordination and demonstrate a significant leap from isolated smart devices to a coherent, efficient 'system'.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in synthesizing spatio-temporal anomaly detection (UTFP-AD) and semantic transformer networks (TSTNet) from urban traffic and IoV domains into a unified control framework for generic consumer IoT, a largely unexplored translation. This moves beyond traditional cloud-centric IoT management to a truly distributed, graph-based 'control' paradigm.

proposals.title: A Self-Triggered, Fault-Adaptive Control Framework for Stochastic Jump Systems with Actuator Saturation
proposals.motivation: Modern networked systems, such as autonomous vehicle platoons and smart grid nodes, operate under stochastic component failures (jumps), physical actuator limits, and evolving hybrid faults. Existing fault-tolerant control (FTC) schemes often rely on periodic execution, wasting communication and computational resources, and lack integrated mechanisms to adapt triggering and control synthesis in real-time based on fault severity, risking instability or performance loss.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating a self-triggered mechanism with a unified fault diagnosis and estimation module will create a closed-loop, resource-aware control architecture that can proactively adjust its conservatism and control signals to maintain stability and performance guarantees for stochastic jump systems under actuator saturation and hybrid faults.
proposals.method: We will model the system as a Markovian jump linear system with actuator saturation and stochastic fault processes. The core architecture will feature: 1) A hybrid observer for simultaneous fault detection, isolation, and estimation (FDI&E) of partial loss, bias, and lock-in-place faults. 2) A self-triggering rule where the next sampling instant is computed based on the current system state and the fault estimation, dynamically relaxing or tightening the trigger threshold. 3) Synthesis of a state-feedback controller using Linear Matrix Inequality (LMI) techniques, incorporating the fault estimates and saturation constraints via a polytopic representation to ensure stochastic stability and optimize a mixed H2/H∞ performance index.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, co-designed control framework that explicitly links fault diagnosis to resource-aware sampling. It will provide formal stability and performance guarantees for a challenging class of systems, advancing the field of resilient cyber-physical systems. The resulting methodology will be directly applicable to resource-constrained networked systems like intelligent transportation and industrial IoT.
proposals.novelty: The closed-loop interdependence between the fault diagnosis module and the self-triggering rule is a key innovation, moving beyond treating fault tolerance and event-triggered control as separate layers. This enables the system to optimally trade off resource usage and control performance in real-time as faults evolve.

proposals.title: A Novel Delay-Product-Type Functional Framework for Robust Stability of RNNs with Unconstrained Time-Varying Delays in Networked AI Systems
proposals.motivation: The deployment of Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) in real-time, latency-sensitive systems like networked vehicle control and distributed AI is hindered by unpredictable, non-differentiable communication delays. Pre-2026 stability analyses often impose restrictive assumptions on delay derivatives or bounds, limiting applicability to real-world scenarios with bursty traffic and unconstrained lag. This gap creates a critical reliability and safety concern for dynamic neural models in the Internet of Vehicles and Industry 5.0 ecosystems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by constructing a novel augmented Lyapunov-Krasovskii functional (LKF) incorporating delay-product terms and leveraging improved integral inequalities (e.g., a refined Wirtinger-based inequality), it is possible to derive delay-dependent stability criteria for RNNs that are less conservative and do not require the delays to be differentiable or slowly varying.
proposals.method: We will first model the RNN as a nonlinear system with time-varying delays constrained only by known upper bounds. The core technical approach involves constructing an augmented LKF with terms like ∫(t-τ(t))^t e^(α(s-t)) ẋᵀ(s)R ẋ(s) ds and cross terms between the state and delayed state. Stability analysis will employ a reciprocally convex combination lemma and a free-weighting matrix technique to handle the delay derivative. The derived linear matrix inequality (LMI) conditions will be solved numerically and validated via simulation of RNNs controlling vehicle platoons under simulated network delays.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a robust theoretical framework with numerically verifiable LMI conditions for certifying RNN stability under realistic delay patterns. It will directly advance the safety verification of AI-driven real-time systems, such as those in autonomous driving and distributed computing networks. The results will be presented as readily applicable theorems for control theorists and AI system designers.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the specific construction of the delay-product-type LKF and its combination with the latest integral inequality techniques to completely remove the common pre-2026 constraint that the time-varying delay τ(t) must satisfy τ̇(t) < 1 or be differentiable, thereby addressing a more general and practical class of delay problems.

proposals.title: Resource-Aware Multi-Robot Coordination via Event-Triggered Control and Delayed Neural Networks: A Framework for Heterogeneous Swarms
proposals.motivation: The deployment of large-scale, heterogeneous multi-robot systems (MRS) in dynamic environments like urban traffic or disaster response faces critical challenges from limited communication bandwidth, computational resources, and inherent network-induced delays. Traditional time-triggered control and coordination schemes generate excessive network traffic, leading to congestion and inefficiency, which severely limits scalability and real-world feasibility. This work addresses the need for a control paradigm that ensures robust, brain-inspired coordination while being fundamentally resource-aware.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that fusing event-triggered control mechanisms with delayed recurrent neural network (DRNN) stability theory can yield a novel coordination framework that guarantees system stability and performance for heterogeneous MRS while drastically reducing communication and computational resource consumption compared to continuous-update schemes.
proposals.method: We will design a hierarchical control architecture where a high-level DRNN, modeled using differential equations with time-varying delays, captures the nonlinear coordination dynamics and predicts swarm behavior. Stability will be analyzed using Lyapunov-Krasovskii functionals. A distributed, threshold-based event-triggering rule will be derived from this stability analysis, dictating when individual robots must broadcast their state to neighbors. The protocol will be validated in simulation for a swarm of heterogeneous ground and aerial robots performing a cooperative transport task under communication constraints.
proposals.contribution: This research will contribute a formally stable, resource-aware control framework for complex MRS, directly applicable to IoV swarms and human-robot teams. It will provide a novel methodology for co-designing communication protocols and neural coordination controllers. The results will demonstrate a significant reduction in network traffic and computational load, enabling the practical use of advanced AI coordination in bandwidth-limited scenarios.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integration of event-triggered mechanisms directly into the stability proof of a delayed neural coordination network, creating a unified design where the communication protocol is intrinsically derived from the neural dynamics' stability requirements, rather than being a separate, heuristic layer.

proposals.title: Event-Triggered Synchronization Control for Delayed Neural Networks in Internet of Vehicles: A Communication-Efficient Framework
proposals.motivation: In the Internet of Vehicles (IoV), neural network-based controllers for autonomous driving and traffic management require constant state updates, generating immense communication overhead. This is exacerbated by inherent network delays, which can degrade control performance and system stability. Pre-2026 research in networked control systems highlights the need for communication-efficient strategies to make large-scale, real-time IoV applications feasible.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying event-triggered control (ETC) strategies, where communication and control updates occur only when a predefined error threshold is exceeded, can guarantee asymptotic synchronization of delayed neural networks while significantly reducing the required communication bandwidth compared to traditional time-triggered schemes.
proposals.method: We will design a Lyapunov-Krasovskii functional for a class of delayed recurrent neural networks modeling vehicle platoon dynamics or traffic flow prediction. A novel event-triggering condition, based on the norm of the synchronization error, will be derived to determine transmission instants. The control law will be synthesized using linear matrix inequality (LMI) techniques to ensure stability under the event-triggered protocol and known delay bounds. The framework will be validated via simulation using SUMO traffic data and realistic V2X communication models.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal stability-guaranteed ETC framework specifically for delayed neural networks within IoV systems, directly reducing communication load. It will extend the application of networked control theory to neural computing models in intelligent transportation, offering a practical design methodology for resource-constrained vehicular networks.
proposals.novelty: While ETC is established for linear networked systems, its application to the nonlinear dynamics of delayed neural networks for IoV control is novel. The integration of temporal semantics (from TSTNet) and stability analysis under sporadic, event-driven updates for neural synchronization presents a significant advancement over periodic control paradigms.

proposals.title: Lyapunov-Shielded Deep Reinforcement Learning for Provably Robust Traffic Control Under Stochastic Jumps and Communication Delays
proposals.motivation: The deployment of Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) for real-time control in complex systems like Internet of Vehicles (IoV) traffic management is hindered by a lack of formal safety and stability guarantees, especially under realistic stochastic disturbances like sudden traffic flow jumps and variable network delays. Pre-2026, methods combining learning with control-theoretic certificates exist but often lack scalability or are too conservative for dynamic, high-dimensional environments.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating a learned Lyapunov function verifier with a DRL agent via a runtime shielding mechanism will yield a control policy that maintains high performance in complex stochastic jump-delay environments while providing provable stability guarantees, ensuring all system trajectories remain within a safe region.
proposals.method: We propose a two-layer architecture: a primary DRL agent (e.g., a Soft Actor-Critic variant) learns a performant policy from spatiotemporal traffic data. In parallel, a neural network learns a candidate Lyapunov function for the system's dynamics, including modeled delay and jump processes. A runtime shield, inspired by control barrier functions, will intercept the DRL agent's proposed actions at each timestep, permitting only those actions that satisfy the Lyapunov stability condition (ΔV(x) < 0). The system will be validated in a high-fidelity traffic simulator with injected stochastic delays and anomalous flow patterns.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, hybrid control framework that bridges the gap between data-driven learning and formal verification for cyber-physical systems. It will provide a concrete methodology for deploying robust, certifiable DRL controllers in safety-critical domains like autonomous vehicle coordination and urban traffic flow management, directly addressing the challenges of stochastic delays and anomalies.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the co-design and online integration of a DRL policy with a learned Lyapunov verifier specifically for stochastic jump-delay systems, moving beyond deterministic or purely delay-ignorant shielding approaches common in pre-2026 safe RL literature.

proposals.title: Deep Meta-Reinforcement Learning for Robust Traffic Control under Stochastic Regime Shifts and Actuation Delays
proposals.motivation: Modern urban traffic and networked control systems face inherent challenges from stochastic disturbances, sudden congestion events (regime shifts), and non-negligible communication/actuation delays. Pre-2026 control methods often struggle with these coupled, partially observable uncertainties, leading to suboptimal performance and reduced robustness in real-time applications like Internet of Vehicles.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that formulating the control problem as a Partially Observable Markov Decision Process (POMDP) and employing a deep meta-reinforcement learning agent to maintain and act on a belief state over hidden system modes and delays will yield a policy robust to stochastic jumps and delays, outperforming conventional and standard deep RL controllers.
proposals.method: We will adapt the POMDP framework from regime-switching portfolio optimization to a traffic/control context. The core method involves training a deep meta-RL agent (e.g., based on recurrent architectures like LSTMs or attention-based transformers) to encode a history of delayed observations and actions into a belief state. This belief will represent the posterior distribution over the active system dynamics mode and the delay state. The agent, trained via algorithms like RL^2 or Probabilistic Embeddings for Actor-Critic RL, will learn to approximate the optimal control policy that maps this belief to actions, enabling robustness without explicit delay or mode identification.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, transferable control paradigm for systems with joint stochasticity and delays, directly inspired by cross-domain analogies. It will provide a practical deep RL-based solution for robust control in smart city and IoV applications, advancing beyond traditional adaptive control and heuristic delay-compensation methods.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit cross-domain transfer of a financial regime-switching POMDP solution to engineering control, and the integration of meta-RL principles to learn a belief-based policy that generalizes across different jump and delay statistics, rather than being tailored to a fixed model.

proposals.title: DRiVe-Net: Dynamic Recurrent Policy Networks for Robust Traffic Signal Control in Stochastic Urban Networks with Delays
proposals.motivation: Urban traffic systems are stochastic hybrid systems with delays, where discrete signal phases and continuous vehicle flows interact under noisy, delayed observations. Pre-2026 Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) for traffic control often uses static policy networks, which struggle to model temporal dependencies and latent mode transitions caused by events like accidents or demand surges, leading to poor generalization. Robust control in such delayed, jumpy environments remains a critical challenge for intelligent transportation systems.
proposals.hypothesis: Replacing a static DRL policy network with a dynamic recurrent neural network (RNN) that maintains an internal state will enable an agent to actively model temporal dependencies and latent mode transitions, leading to more robust control policies that generalize better across unseen delay patterns and stochastic disturbances in hybrid traffic systems.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel DRL framework based on Soft Actor-Critic (SAC). The core innovation is a dynamic recurrent policy network, implemented using Gated Recurrent Units (GRUs) and compared to Liquid Time-Constant (LTC) networks, which processes a history of noisy observations and actions. This network's hidden state will serve as a learned filter for delays and a predictor of hybrid mode transitions. The agent will be trained and evaluated in a high-fidelity simulation environment (e.g., SUMO) modeling a stochastic urban network with variable observation/action delays and randomized incident patterns.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel DRL architecture specifically designed for delayed, stochastic hybrid systems, advancing beyond feedforward and simple recurrent baseline policies. It will provide new empirical evidence on the efficacy of dynamic internal states for robust control, with direct applications to adaptive traffic signal control and, by extension, other networked cyber-physical systems with similar challenges.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit fusion of a dynamic, stateful RNN policy within a DRL loop to concurrently address observation noise, action delays, and latent mode jumps in a unified learning framework, moving beyond robust-but-static policies or separate state estimation modules common in pre-2026 work.

proposals.title: Deep Reinforcement Learning for Robust Control in Stochastic Hybrid Traffic Systems with Delays
proposals.motivation: Urban traffic and Internet of Vehicles (IoV) systems are complex, hybrid dynamical systems subject to stochastic mode jumps (e.g., sudden congestion, accidents) and inherent communication/actuation delays. Pre-2026 control methods often struggle to maintain stability and performance under such combined, non-stationary disturbances, posing a significant challenge for real-time traffic management and autonomous driving coordination.
proposals.hypothesis: A deep reinforcement learning (DRL) agent, trained within a simulated environment that explicitly models stochastic Markovian jumping modes and variable time delays, can learn a control policy that is robust to these disturbances and outperforms conventional model-based or delay-ignorant controllers.
proposals.method: We will model the traffic control system as a Markov Jump Linear System (MJLS) with bounded, variable delays. A Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (DDPG) or Twin Delayed DDPG (TD3) agent will be the core learner due to their suitability for continuous action spaces. The agent's state observation will be augmented with a history buffer to implicitly learn delay compensation, and the training environment will randomly switch system dynamics (modes) according to a transition probability matrix to enforce robustness.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel DRL-based control framework specifically designed for stochastic hybrid systems with delays. It will provide a validated, data-driven alternative to complex analytical robust control design for urban traffic networks and IoV applications, directly enhancing system resilience as targeted in Industry 5.0 paradigms.
proposals.novelty: The integration of DRL with formal MJLS-delay models for control is non-trivial. While DRL has been applied to traffic, its explicit co-design with jumping parameters and delays for robust policy synthesis remains an open and challenging problem pre-2026.

proposals.title: STARL-SC: A Self-Triggered Adaptive Reinforcement Learning Framework for Soft Robotic Control under Stochastic Network Delays
proposals.motivation: The deployment of soft robots in unstructured, real-world environments like healthcare or exploration necessitates robust control over communication networks with inherent stochastic delays, a challenge well-studied in vehicular networks and IoT systems. Traditional time-triggered control is inefficient for these resource-constrained, continuously deformable systems, while existing event-triggered schemes with fixed thresholds cannot adapt to changing delay distributions and nonlinear soft body dynamics. This gap limits their autonomy and energy efficiency in unpredictable settings.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a reinforcement learning agent can co-adapt the parameters of a stochastic event-triggering condition and an underlying fuzzy control policy, leading to a self-triggered framework that optimally balances soft robot task performance with communication and actuation resource usage under observed delay patterns.
proposals.method: We will model stochastic delays using a Markov chain, integrated into a soft robot dynamics simulator. A Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (DDPG) agent will serve as the meta-controller, with its action space defining adjustments to both the triggering condition's threshold and the consequent part of a Takagi-Sugeno fuzzy controller. The agent's reward will combine task error, triggering frequency, and a penalty for delay-induced instability. Training will occur in progressively more stochastic delay environments to learn a robust policy.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, adaptive self-triggered control architecture specifically for soft robotics, directly addressing network-induced uncertainty. It will provide a methodology for the joint optimization of control and communication policies, advancing beyond static event-triggering. The resulting framework is expected to demonstrate significant gains in energy efficiency and robustness in simulation-based validation.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the dual RL-based tuning of both the event-triggering mechanism and the control rules, creating a synergistic, fully adaptive loop tailored for soft robots—a significant departure from applying fixed network control strategies to rigid systems or tuning control policies alone.

proposals.title: Co-Design of Self-Triggered Output-Feedback Control and Online Markovian Delay Estimation for Networked Systems
proposals.motivation: Networked control systems, such as those in Internet of Vehicles and sensor networks, suffer from variable, network-induced delays that degrade stability and performance. Existing self-triggered control schemes often assume known delay statistics or require full-state feedback, limiting their applicability in real-world scenarios with partial observations and unknown, time-varying channel conditions. This creates a critical gap for resource-constrained remote control over unreliable links.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a co-designed framework, which jointly estimates the latent system state and the active Markovian delay mode from intermittent, noisy output samples, can stabilize a system without continuous sensing or a priori delay dynamics, and that the self-triggering mechanism can be actively tuned to improve delay mode identification.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel observer that fuses an Interacting Multiple Model (IMM) estimator for the Markovian delay mode with a switched linear Kalman filter for the system state, using only the triggered output samples. The self-triggering law will be derived from a stochastic Lyapunov function, with the next triggering instant computed based on the current state and delay mode estimates. The estimator and trigger will be co-optimized using a min-max optimization to balance control performance, communication cost, and estimation accuracy.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical control scheme for networked systems with unknown, stochastic delays and output feedback, eliminating the need for continuous sensing. It will contribute a new theoretical framework for the joint optimization of estimation, triggering, and control under partial information, directly applicable to traffic control and autonomous vehicle platooning over wireless networks.
proposals.novelty: The closed-loop interdependence where the triggering condition actively probes the network to reduce delay mode uncertainty, while the improved delay estimate refines the trigger and control, is a novel co-design principle not found in existing separate estimation or control-triggering literature.

proposals.title: Federated Spatiotemporal Transformer for Privacy-Preserving Genomic Language Model Training
proposals.motivation: The analysis of sensitive genomic data using large language models (LLMs) is hindered by strict privacy regulations and the logistical challenges of centralizing massive, distributed datasets. Pre-2026, federated learning (FL) has emerged as a key paradigm for distributed AI in domains like IoT and healthcare, but its application to complex, sequential genomic data for LLM training remains underexplored. This creates a critical bottleneck for advancing personalized medicine and genomic research while preserving data sovereignty.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a federated learning framework, built upon a spatiotemporal transformer architecture, can effectively train a robust genomic LLM on distributed, sensitive data without centralization, achieving predictive performance comparable to centralized training while providing formal privacy guarantees.
proposals.method: We will adapt the Temporal Semantic Transformer (TSTNet) architecture, proven for sequential IoT data, to model genomic sequences as temporal signals. A federated averaging (FedAvg) protocol will coordinate training across multiple institutional clients (e.g., hospitals, biobanks). To address non-IID data and communication delays—challenges noted in vehicular FL research—we will integrate client selection strategies and adaptive aggregation weights. Differential privacy will be added at the client level during model update transmission to provide rigorous privacy assurances.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, privacy-by-design framework for distributed genomic AI, enabling collaborative LLM training previously infeasible due to privacy constraints. It will advance the field of federated learning by adapting and testing spatiotemporal models for a new, critical data modality, and provide a concrete benchmark for privacy-utility trade-offs in genomic FL.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the fusion of a spatiotemporal transformer (TSTNet), designed for vehicular time-series, with a robust FL scheme to tackle the unique sequential and privacy challenges of genomic data, a direct cross-pollination of methods from Vasilakos's prior work in traffic systems to genomics.

proposals.title: Transfer Learning from Classical CNNs for Enhanced Spatiotemporal Anomaly Detection in Urban Traffic Systems
proposals.motivation: Urban traffic anomaly detection is critical for intelligent transportation systems, yet real-time analysis of spatiotemporal data remains challenging due to dynamic patterns and computational constraints. Hybrid networks combining classical and novel architectures (e.g., Transformers) show promise but often suffer from slow convergence and require large, labeled datasets, which are scarce for specific urban scenarios. Leveraging pre-existing, robust feature extractors can address these training inefficiencies.
proposals.hypothesis: Initializing the classical convolutional components of a hybrid spatiotemporal network (e.g., CNN-Transformer) with weights from a model pre-trained on large-scale, general image datasets will significantly improve convergence speed, detection accuracy, and robustness in data-scarce urban traffic anomaly prediction tasks.
proposals.method: We propose a hybrid TSTNet-like architecture where the initial spatial feature extraction is performed by a classical CNN backbone (e.g., ResNet-50). This backbone will be initialized with weights pre-trained on ImageNet. The extracted spatial features will then be fed into a temporal semantic transformer module to model complex spatiotemporal dependencies in urban traffic flow data. The entire network will be fine-tuned on spatiotemporal urban traffic datasets (e.g., PeMS, TaxiBJ) for the specific downstream task of flow pattern anomaly detection, as defined in UTFP-AD.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated framework for efficient transfer learning in spatiotemporal traffic analysis, reducing data and computational requirements for training robust models. It will demonstrate how foundational computer vision models can be repurposed to accelerate convergence and improve performance in intelligent transportation systems, contributing to more reliable real-time traffic control.
proposals.novelty: While transfer learning is common in vision, its systematic application to initialize the classical components of emerging hybrid spatiotemporal networks for traffic analytics is novel. This approach strategically bridges well-established convolutional feature hierarchies with cutting-edge temporal attention mechanisms, a synergy not fully explored in pre-2026 IoV research.

proposals.title: A Spatiotemporal QCNN Framework for Urban Traffic Forecasting Enhanced by Real-Time Weather and Event Data
proposals.motivation: Current urban traffic forecasting models, including Quantum Convolutional Neural Networks (QCNN), often rely on historical traffic data, neglecting dynamic external factors that cause significant, non-recurrent congestion. Pre-2026, the integration of real-time, multi-source external data into advanced spatiotemporal models remains a key challenge for improving prediction accuracy and enabling proactive traffic control in smart city systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating real-time weather forecasts and scheduled local event data as exogenous spatiotemporal channels into a QCNN-based forecasting framework will significantly improve the accuracy and robustness of short-term urban traffic flow predictions compared to models using only historical traffic data.
proposals.method: We will extend a baseline spatiotemporal QCNN architecture by designing a multi-modal data fusion module. Real-time weather data (precipitation, visibility) and event calendars will be encoded into spatiotemporal grids aligned with traffic sensor networks. A Temporal Semantic Transformer (TSTNet-like) encoder will process these dynamic external sequences, and its output will be fused with the QCNN's internal representations via an attention-based gating mechanism. The integrated model will be trained and evaluated on real-world urban datasets (e.g., PeMS) using metrics like MAE and RMSE.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, hybrid quantum-classical neural architecture for traffic forecasting that explicitly models the impact of dynamic externalities. It will provide a practical framework for integrating heterogeneous real-time data streams into spatiotemporal prediction systems, advancing the field of intelligent transportation systems (ITS) towards more resilient and context-aware control.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the specific fusion mechanism between a QCNN for core spatiotemporal traffic patterns and a transformer-based encoder for dynamic external semantics, creating a unified model that addresses the real-time integration challenge of volatile external factors not deeply explored in prior QCNN traffic research.

proposals.title: Federated Quantum Convolutional Neural Networks for Privacy-Preserving Smart Grid Load Forecasting
proposals.motivation: Accurate short-term load forecasting (STLF) is critical for grid stability and efficient energy management in smart grids. However, centralized training on consumer smart meter data raises severe privacy and data security concerns, creating a significant barrier to leveraging distributed data sources. Federated learning (FL) offers a paradigm for collaborative model training without raw data exchange, but its application to complex, temporal load patterns remains a challenge.
proposals.hypothesis: A Federated Learning framework built upon a Quantum Convolutional Neural Network (QCNN) architecture can achieve forecasting accuracy comparable to centralized models while preserving consumer data privacy and capturing complex spatiotemporal dependencies in load profiles.
proposals.method: We propose a novel FL system where each smart meter (client) trains a local QCNN model on its private, temporal load data. The QCNN will utilize parameterized quantum circuits as convolutional filters to process time-series data. A central aggregator will employ the Federated Averaging (FedAvg) algorithm to synchronize model parameters. We will integrate differential privacy mechanisms at the client level during gradient uploads and design a client selection strategy based on data quality and temporal patterns to mitigate the non-IID data challenge inherent in FL.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, privacy-by-design framework for STLF that directly addresses data sovereignty regulations. It will advance the application of quantum machine learning in practical, distributed systems and provide new insights into handling non-IID, temporal data in federated settings for critical infrastructure.
proposals.novelty: The integration of a QCNN for temporal feature extraction within a federated learning framework is novel, combining the representational advantages of quantum models with the privacy guarantees of FL for a critical smart grid application, a synergy not explored in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Temporal Load Forecasting in Smart Grids via a Quantum-Inspired Transformer with Spatiotemporal Attention
proposals.motivation: Accurate forecasting of electricity load patterns is critical for grid stability and efficient resource allocation in smart cities, especially with the increasing integration of intermittent renewable sources. Existing deep learning models, including Quantum Convolutional Neural Networks (QCNNs), often struggle to capture the complex, long-range temporal dependencies and non-linear spatiotemporal correlations inherent in smart meter and grid sensor data.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a quantum-inspired transformer architecture, leveraging multi-head self-attention mechanisms, will outperform classical and simple quantum-inspired feed-forward models in forecasting accuracy by more effectively modeling long-term temporal dependencies and dynamic correlations in multivariate load time-series data.
proposals.method: We will design a Quantum-Inspired Transformer (QIT) model where the core self-attention mechanism operates on classically encoded quantum state representations (e.g., using amplitude or angle encoding of time-series features). The model will incorporate positional encoding for temporal order and a spatiotemporal attention block, inspired by Vasilakos's prior work on urban traffic patterns, to jointly attend to temporal lags and spatial correlations from different grid nodes. Training will utilize a classical optimizer on simulated and real-world smart grid datasets to predict short-term (24-hour) load profiles.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, hybrid quantum-classical architecture for spatiotemporal forecasting, demonstrating the utility of attention mechanisms for complex system dynamics beyond NLP. It will provide a new benchmark model for temporal load forecasting and offer insights into the integration of quantum-inspired representations with advanced neural network components for systems control applications.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the specific fusion of a quantum-inspired feature encoding with a transformer's self-attention mechanism for a systems engineering problem (smart grid load forecasting), moving beyond the application of QCNNs or pure classical transformers in this domain.

proposals.title: Robust Self-Triggered Control for Networked Systems Under Stochastic Packet Loss: A Stochastic Hybrid Systems Approach
proposals.motivation: Self-triggered control (STC) is a key method for reducing communication and computation in networked control systems, such as connected vehicles and IoT-based traffic management. However, existing STC stability analyses often assume ideal, lossless communication channels, which is unrealistic in real-world wireless networks like IoV. Ignoring packet loss can lead to degraded performance or even instability in safety-critical applications.
proposals.hypothesis: By explicitly modeling packet loss as a stochastic process within a stochastic hybrid systems (SHS) framework, we can derive new, less conservative triggering conditions and stability guarantees that ensure mean-square stability (MSS) or safety with a high probability for the networked control loop.
proposals.method: We will model the closed-loop system with STC and a Bernoulli or Markovian packet loss process as a SHS. Using Lyapunov-based techniques tailored to SHS, we will derive stability conditions that jointly govern the triggering rule and the maximum tolerable loss probability. The analysis will be validated via co-simulation (e.g., SUMO for traffic and NS-3 for network) on a platooning or intersection control case study, comparing our robust STC against conventional STC under simulated 4G/5G V2X channel impairments.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal stability analysis framework for STC under packet loss, bridging a gap between communication theory and control theory. It will yield practical, robust triggering laws that can be deployed in real IoV and CPN systems to maintain performance despite unreliable links.
proposals.novelty: While packet loss in event-triggered control has been studied, its integration into the predictive and deterministic nature of self-triggered control—where the next update time is computed *before* receiving new data—poses a distinct and largely unaddressed challenge requiring a novel stochastic formulation.

proposals.title: Resilient Self-Triggered Control for Multi-Link Flexible Robots: A Co-Design for Vibration Damping and Network Loss Compensation
proposals.motivation: The deployment of flexible robotic manipulators in networked industrial and teleoperated settings (Industry 4.0/5.0) is hampered by two coupled challenges: inherent mechanical vibrations and unreliable communication inducing data loss. Existing control strategies often treat these issues separately, leading to performance degradation or excessive communication overhead, which limits scalability and robustness in real-world applications like automated assembly or remote surgery.
proposals.hypothesis: A unified self-triggered control law, co-designed with a distributed flexibility model, can actively compensate for lost sensor packets and jointly stabilize link vibrations while significantly reducing network and actuation resource usage compared to periodic or standard event-triggered schemes.
proposals.method: We will develop a control architecture integrating a spatiotemporal model of the flexible multi-link dynamics (based on assumed modes or finite element method) with a resilient self-triggering rule. The rule will compute the next actuation update time based on predicted state evolution during anticipated packet loss intervals, using Lyapunov-Krasovskii functionals for stability analysis under network uncertainties. Validation will be conducted via high-fidelity simulation (MATLAB/Simulink) and a physical multi-link flexible robot testbed with an emulated lossy network layer.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal control-theoretic framework for resilient operation of distributed flexible systems under network degradation. It will deliver a practical co-design methodology for communication scheduling and vibration control, reducing wear and energy use. The experimental benchmarks will set a new standard for robustness in networked soft robotics.
proposals.novelty: The explicit integration of a distributed flexibility model into the self-triggering mechanism to compensate for data loss, rather than merely tolerating it, represents a significant departure from existing network control systems which typically treat the plant as a lumped-parameter system.

proposals.title: Learning to Trigger: Online Adaptive Optimization of Event-Triggered Control for Networked Traffic Systems
proposals.motivation: Event-triggered control (ETC) is crucial for resource-constrained systems like the Internet of Vehicles (IoV), reducing communication and computation by transmitting data only when necessary. However, pre-2026 ETC schemes often rely on static, conservatively tuned triggering thresholds, which can lead to performance degradation or unnecessary resource use under dynamic traffic conditions. There is a need for self-optimizing control frameworks that can adapt triggering behavior in real-time to balance system stability, traffic flow efficiency, and network resource consumption.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating online adaptive control techniques, specifically a concurrent learning-based parameter update law, with a spatiotemporal traffic dynamics model will allow the ETC triggering condition to self-optimize, leading to superior trade-offs between control performance and communication savings compared to static ETC in urban traffic management.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-layer architecture. The lower layer implements a distributed ETC for traffic signal coordination or vehicle platooning, using a triggering condition based on a Lyapunov-like function with tunable parameters. The upper layer employs a model-reference adaptive control (MRAC) framework, where a spatiotemporal transformer network (inspired by TSTNet) models the traffic dynamics. A concurrent learning algorithm will continuously update the triggering parameters by minimizing a cost function that weights tracking error against communication rate, using real-time data from connected vehicles and infrastructure.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel adaptive event-triggered control (AETC) framework specifically designed for dynamic, large-scale traffic systems. It will provide a methodological advance in co-designing control and communication policies for IoV. The results are expected to demonstrate quantifiable reductions in network delays and control effort while maintaining or improving traffic flow metrics like throughput and queue length.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the closed-loop, online learning of the ETC triggering rule itself within a complex spatiotemporal system, moving beyond offline tuning or heuristic adjustments. This merges adaptive control theory with real-time learning in networked traffic systems, a direction not fully explored in pre-2026 literature on ETC for IoV.

proposals.title: Robust Adaptive Event-Triggered MPC for Connected Autonomous Vehicles with Online Uncertainty Quantification
proposals.motivation: Event-triggered control via Model Predictive Control (MPC) is a promising paradigm for resource-constrained systems like Connected Autonomous Vehicles (CAVs), reducing communication and computation by triggering actions only when necessary. However, its performance and stability are critically fragile to model inaccuracies and disturbances inherent in complex, real-world traffic environments. Pre-2026 literature lacks a unified framework that simultaneously adapts the nominal model online and robustly plans triggering instants against quantified uncertainty, limiting the reliability of CAV platooning and intersection control.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating an online recursive system identification process with a robust MPC formulation that optimizes over a dynamically updated uncertainty set will yield a self-correcting event-triggered controller that maintains safety and performance guarantees under significant model drift and disturbances.
proposals.method: We will develop a dual-loop architecture. The outer loop employs a Recursive Least Squares (RLS) algorithm with forgetting factor to continuously update a linear parameter-varying vehicle dynamics model from streaming V2X data. The inner loop formulates a min-max robust MPC problem, where the uncertainty set (e.g., polytopic or ellipsoidal) is defined by the online-estimated error covariance from the RLS. This optimization will solve for the worst-case cost over this set to determine the next optimal triggering instant and control input, using tube-based MPC techniques to ensure constraint satisfaction.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formally grounded control framework that enhances the resilience of CAV coordination against unpredictable traffic dynamics. It will contribute a novel algorithm merging online learning and robust optimization specifically for event-triggered systems, advancing towards 'self-healing' cyber-physical transportation systems.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the tight, online coupling of model adaptation and uncertainty set quantification specifically for the event-triggering prediction horizon, moving beyond static robust designs or adaptation schemes decoupled from the robustness guarantee.

proposals.title: Predictive Patch-Leaving for Resource-Aware Control: An MPC-Based Event-Triggered Framework Inspired by Optimal Foraging Theory
proposals.motivation: In large-scale cyber-physical systems like Internet of Vehicles (IoV) and smart traffic control, frequent communication for control updates creates bottlenecks, delays, and resource strain. Existing event-triggered control (ETC) schemes often rely on heuristic or instantaneous thresholds, lacking a predictive, long-term optimization of the communication-performance trade-off. This limits their adaptability in dynamic, resource-constrained environments prevalent in pre-2026 IoV and Industry 5.0 applications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that framing the ETC problem through the lens of Optimal Foraging Theory (OFT) and solving it with a Model Predictive Control (MPC) framework will yield a triggering policy that proactively optimizes long-term control performance under strict communication constraints, outperforming reactive threshold-based methods.
proposals.method: We will develop a dual-horizon MPC scheme. An inner loop performs standard constrained control optimization. An outer, supervisory loop runs a parallel MPC that predicts a 'resource landscape'—modeling control performance (e.g., state uncertainty) as a depletable patch and communication cost as travel. The triggering condition is derived from a predictive marginal value theorem, scheduling the next update when the predicted net utility of staying falls below that of triggering and re-optimizing. This will be implemented and tested on a co-simulation platform integrating SUMO for traffic flow and a networked control simulator, using real urban mobility datasets.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, bio-inspired algorithmic framework for predictive event-triggered control that explicitly optimizes long-term resource allocation. It will provide a formal methodology to translate ecological foraging strategies into engineering rules for adaptive system communication, directly applicable to IoV and distributed sensor-actuator networks. The results will advance the integration of MPC and ETC beyond static thresholds.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct conceptual and formal analogy to Optimal Foraging Theory to re-formulate the triggering problem, and the synthesis of a dual-horizon MPC that proactively schedules events based on predicted resource utility, rather than reacting to immediate state errors.

proposals.title: MPC-Trigger: Model Predictive Control for Adaptive Event-Triggered Communication in Cooperative Autonomous Vehicle Platoons
proposals.motivation: In the Internet of Vehicles (IoV), efficient communication is critical for platoon control but faces challenges from network congestion and resource constraints. Traditional periodic or simple event-triggered schemes can lead to excessive communication delays or unnecessary network load, degrading safety and traffic flow. An intelligent, predictive triggering mechanism is needed to optimize communication instants in real-time.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a Model Predictive Control (MPC) framework, which predicts future vehicle states and network conditions, can optimally schedule communication-triggering instants. This will maintain platoon stability and string safety while significantly reducing network resource usage compared to state-of-the-art event-triggered control.
proposals.method: We will develop a hierarchical control architecture. At the lower level, a vehicle platoon operates under a standard consensus-based controller. The novel upper layer is an MPC-based triggering scheduler. This scheduler uses a spatiotemporal prediction model (inspired by TSTNet's transformer for temporal semantics) to forecast vehicle trajectories and potential disturbances over a receding horizon. It then solves a constrained optimization problem at each step to determine the next optimal triggering instant, balancing control performance (e.g., spacing error) against a communication cost. The framework will be validated using SUMO/OMNeT++ co-simulation with real-world traffic datasets.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel MPC-based adaptive triggering protocol for IoV platoons, directly reducing network overhead without compromising safety. It will provide a formal methodology to co-design control and communication scheduling, advancing the field of cyber-physical systems integration for autonomous driving.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying MPC not for direct vehicle control, but explicitly for optimizing the *timing* of communication events in a networked control system. This moves beyond reactive triggering to proactive, schedule-aware communication, a significant shift from existing event-triggered control literature.

proposals.title: Safe Online Tuning of Fuzzy Traffic Controllers via Shielded Reinforcement Learning and a Synchronized Digital Twin
proposals.motivation: Adaptive fuzzy control systems are crucial for managing complex, dynamic urban traffic flows, but their online optimization via Reinforcement Learning (RL) poses significant safety risks. Unconstrained RL exploration can lead to catastrophic control actions, causing real-world traffic delays or instability. A framework that guarantees safety during online learning is essential for deploying adaptive control in critical infrastructure like the Internet of Vehicles.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating Control Barrier Functions (CBFs) as a real-time safety filter with a high-fidelity digital twin for pre-evaluation will enable safe, stable, and accelerated online RL-based tuning of fuzzy rule bases for urban traffic control.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-layer architecture. The upper layer uses a Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (DDPG) agent to propose adjustments to a Takagi-Sugeno fuzzy controller's rule consequents. Every action is passed through a CBF-based safety shield that mathematically certifies constraint satisfaction (e.g., queue length limits) before deployment. A synchronized digital twin, built on a microscopic traffic simulator (e.g., SUMO), runs in parallel, allowing proposed policies to be pre-evaluated and the RL agent to be trained on synthetic yet high-fidelity data, reducing risky real-world exploration.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, safety-guaranteed framework for the online adaptation of fuzzy traffic control systems. It will demonstrate a practical integration of CBFs, RL, and digital twins for critical system tuning. The result will be a methodology that accelerates convergence to robust control policies while entirely preventing constraint violations during the learning process.
proposals.novelty: The novel synthesis of a CBF safety shield with a digital twin for safe RL exploration specifically applied to fuzzy rule tuning in traffic systems is non-obvious. It moves beyond offline tuning or simulation-only RL by enabling certified safe online learning in the physical world.

proposals.title: MARL-FRTC: A Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning Framework for Adaptive Fuzzy Rule Tuning in Self-Triggered Networked Control Systems
proposals.motivation: Self-triggered control (STC) is crucial for resource-constrained systems like Internet of Vehicles (IoV) and smart grids, as it reduces communication and computation by predicting necessary actuation times. However, designing and tuning the fuzzy rule base for STC is complex, especially under dynamic network conditions with delays and stochastic disturbances. Existing methods lack the ability for online, autonomous adaptation of the rule base to maintain performance and efficiency.
proposals.hypothesis: Modeling fuzzy rules as cooperative agents in a Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL) framework will enable the self-triggered controller to autonomously and dynamically tune its rule parameters, leading to superior adaptability and resilience in networked control systems compared to static or manually tuned fuzzy STC.
proposals.method: We propose a framework where each fuzzy rule (or a cluster) is an independent agent. Agents use a centralized training with decentralized execution (CTDE) MARL paradigm, specifically a multi-agent variant of Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (MADDPG), suitable for continuous action spaces (e.g., tuning membership function parameters). The shared reward function incorporates global tracking error, settling time, and a penalty for excessive triggering. The agents' policies are trained in a simulated environment modeling networked control with stochastic delays, inspired by pre-2026 traffic and IoV simulation platforms.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, adaptive fuzzy STC architecture that autonomously optimizes control performance and resource efficiency. It will provide a methodological bridge between MARL from ecological management and intelligent control systems, demonstrating improved robustness in simulations of IoV platooning or traffic signal control scenarios with real-world delays.
proposals.novelty: The novel integration of a cooperative MARL structure for the distributed, online tuning of a fuzzy inference system, specifically within a self-triggered control context, is unexplored. This moves beyond using single-agent RL for parameter tuning by enabling collaborative rule adaptation among agents.

proposals.title: Inverse Optimal Control-Guided Reinforcement Learning for Adaptive Self-Triggered Fuzzy Control in Networked Systems with Stochastic Delays
proposals.motivation: Modern networked control systems, such as Internet of Vehicles and Industry 5.0 applications, face significant challenges from stochastic communication delays that degrade stability and performance. While self-triggered fuzzy control offers a robust baseline, its rule base is often static and sub-optimal under dynamic network conditions. There is a critical need for controllers that can adaptively self-improve while maintaining interpretability and stability guarantees, bridging the gap between data-driven learning and formal control theory.
proposals.hypothesis: The cost function recovered via Inverse Optimal Control from a stable baseline self-triggered fuzzy controller encodes a meaningful stability criterion, and using this cost as a reward signal will guide Reinforcement Learning to safely and optimally tune the controller's fuzzy rules, ensuring improved performance without compromising system stability.
proposals.method: First, a baseline Self-Triggered Fuzzy Logic Controller (ST-FLC) will be designed for a networked system model with stochastic delays. Using observed state-control trajectories from this stable baseline, an Inverse Optimal Control (IOC) method, such as a max-margin or maximum entropy IRL algorithm, will be employed to recover a quadratic-like cost function. This identified cost function will then define the reward for a Reinforcement Learning agent (e.g., using Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient or Soft Actor-Critic) tasked with dynamically adjusting the parameters of the fuzzy rule consequents, creating a closed-loop, self-improving control architecture.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel adaptive control framework that merges the interpretability of fuzzy systems with the optimization power of RL, grounded by IOC-derived stability guarantees. It will deliver a methodology for creating self-tuning controllers resilient to network-induced stochastic delays, applicable to cyber-physical systems like automated driving and smart infrastructure.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the use of Inverse Optimal Control not merely for explanation, but as a formal bridge to generate a stability-aware reward function for RL-based tuning, ensuring that data-driven adaptation is inherently aligned with the system's fundamental stability properties, a synergy not explored in prior fuzzy or RL control literature.

proposals.title: Asynchronous Self-Triggered Control for Multi-Agent Flexible Robotic Networks with Unmatched Premise Variables
proposals.motivation: Modern collaborative robotic systems, such as in smart manufacturing or search-and-rescue, require resilient coordination under communication constraints and hardware heterogeneity. Traditional event-triggered and self-triggered control schemes for multi-agent systems often rely on restrictive assumptions like matched premise variables, limiting their applicability to real-world flexible-joint robots with asynchronous, independent local dynamics and communication delays. This gap hinders the deployment of efficient, resource-aware control in dynamic Industrial IoT and vehicular network environments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by formulating a novel distributed control law and self-triggering condition based on local Lyapunov functions and neighbor state estimates, a network of flexible-joint robots can achieve stable coordination without requiring matched or constrained-mismatched premise variables, thereby enabling fully asynchronous operation with guaranteed performance.
proposals.method: We will develop a Takagi-Sugeno fuzzy model for each flexible-joint robotic agent, explicitly allowing unmatched premise variables. A distributed self-triggered mechanism will be designed where each agent independently computes its next update time based on its local state and received neighbor estimates, using a stability condition derived via a piecewise Lyapunov functional. The control synthesis will be cast as a Linear Matrix Inequality (LMI) optimization problem to co-design the controller and triggering parameters, ensuring stability despite asynchronous triggers and communication latencies.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a formal stability-guaranteed framework for asynchronous, resource-efficient coordination of heterogeneous robotic networks. It will provide a significant relaxation of the matched premise variable condition, a major limitation in existing fuzzy-model-based networked control. The resulting algorithms will directly reduce communication and computation overhead in collaborative robotic tasks.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the removal of the matched/mismatched premise variable constraint for self-triggered multi-agent systems, enabling truly independent and asynchronous triggering schedules. This integrates flexible-joint robot dynamics with a communication-aware control paradigm in a way not previously achieved in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Co-Design for Delay Robustness: Joint Optimization of Physical System Dynamics and Self-Triggered Control in Cyber-Physical Systems
proposals.motivation: Modern cyber-physical systems (CPS), such as autonomous vehicle platoons and smart grids, face fundamental challenges from communication delays and network-induced uncertainties, which degrade performance and threaten stability. Existing research treats controller/network design and physical plant design separately, missing synergistic opportunities to enhance temporal robustness. This gap is critical in safety-critical applications where guaranteed stability under delayed information is paramount.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a unified co-design framework, which jointly optimizes selectable physical system parameters (e.g., mechanical compliance, actuator placement) and the triggering thresholds of a self-triggered control policy, can systematically maximize the tolerable delay margin beyond what is achievable by optimizing either layer in isolation.
proposals.method: We will formulate a constrained optimization problem where the decision variables are both physical parameters and trigger policy parameters. The objective function will be a metric of delay robustness (e.g., maximum allowable delay, or a robustness margin from small-gain analysis), with constraints on closed-loop stability (via Lyapunov or dissipativity theory) and performance. We will employ a nested optimization approach: an outer loop using gradient-based or evolutionary algorithms (e.g., CMA-ES) to explore the physical design space, and an inner loop that, for a given plant, computes the optimal self-triggered policy using techniques from model predictive control or event-triggered control theory. The framework will be validated in a high-fidelity vehicular platoon simulation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel methodology for the holistic design of delay-resilient CPS, delivering a set of co-design principles. It will yield concrete design guidelines showing how to 'tune' a physical system to be more forgiving of network imperfections. The result is a significant step towards intrinsically robust networked control systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit, optimization-based coupling of traditionally separate physical and cyber design layers to maximize delay robustness, creating a synergistic loop where the plant is engineered to facilitate less frequent, more robust communication, and vice-versa.

proposals.title: A Dynamic Risk-Score Matrix for Connected Vehicles: Integrating Real-Time Threat Intelligence with Spatiotemporal Traffic Anomaly Detection
proposals.motivation: The security of Internet of Vehicles (IoV) systems is critical for safe autonomous driving, yet existing risk assessment models are often static and lack real-time contextual awareness. This limitation is acute in urban traffic systems where cyber-physical threats can cause cascading failures, leading to safety hazards and significant traffic delays. Integrating dynamic, external threat intelligence into risk evaluation is a necessary step towards proactive security in Industry 5.0-inspired vehicular networks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that augmenting a traditional Risk-Score Matrix with curated, real-time threat intelligence feeds will significantly improve the accuracy and timeliness of threat prediction for vehicle-to-everything (V2X) communications, leading to more resilient traffic flow management.
proposals.method: We will extend the UTFP-AD framework by designing a fusion module that ingests structured threat intelligence (e.g., STIX/TAXII feeds) and correlates it with real-time spatiotemporal traffic data. A Temporal Semantic Transformer (TSTNet) core will be adapted to model the temporal dynamics of both traffic patterns and emerging threat indicators. A novel scoring algorithm will dynamically adjust the Risk-Score Matrix weights based on the fused intelligence, triggering automated control responses via a blockchain-anchored access control system for data integrity.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, operational security framework for IoV that closes the loop between threat intelligence and real-time system control. It will provide a methodology for quantifiably assessing how external cyber threats translate to physical traffic anomalies, enhancing the predictive capability of systems like UTFP-AD beyond purely historical traffic data.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the semantic and temporal fusion of external cyber threat intelligence with internal spatiotemporal traffic anomaly detection, creating a context-aware risk model that evolves in real-time, a significant advance over static risk assessment or isolated intrusion detection systems.

proposals.title: ETP-WE: A Framework for Edge Traffic Prediction with Workload Embeddings Validated on Diverse Industrial Cloud Datasets
proposals.motivation: The proliferation of Industrial IoT and real-time applications like autonomous driving demands robust edge computing resource management, where accurate traffic prediction is critical for minimizing delays and ensuring Quality of Service. While frameworks like ETP-WE (Edge Traffic Prediction with Workload Embeddings) show promise on open datasets like OpenNebula and Google Cluster, their generalizability to proprietary, heterogeneous industrial cloud environments remains unproven. Validating such frameworks on diverse, real-world industrial data is essential for their adoption in Industry 5.0 and Internet of Vehicles ecosystems.
proposals.hypothesis: The ETP-WE framework, which integrates temporal semantic modeling of workload patterns, will maintain robust prediction accuracy for edge network traffic when applied to proprietary industrial cloud datasets, demonstrating its generalizability beyond public benchmarks.
proposals.method: We will implement a systematic test framework to validate the ETP-WE core architecture. This involves adapting its Temporal Semantic Transformer (TST) backbone, as used in TSTNet for IoV, to process multi-source spatiotemporal traffic logs from at least two proprietary industrial cloud providers. We will employ the anomaly detection principles from UTFP-AD to pre-process and flag data inconsistencies. The model will be trained to predict traffic loads and resource contention, with performance metrics (e.g., MAE, RMSE) compared against baseline models like LSTMs and standard Transformers on the same proprietary datasets.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first rigorous, cross-domain validation of the ETP-WE framework, establishing its practical utility for industrial edge-cloud systems. It will produce a benchmark of prediction performance on previously unexplored proprietary datasets, offering valuable insights into the real-world challenges of temporal modeling in heterogeneous industrial environments.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies not in a new algorithm, but in the targeted validation methodology using diverse, proprietary industrial data streams, which tests the fundamental assumption of transferability for state-of-the-art temporal prediction frameworks beyond curated academic datasets.

proposals.title: Federated Spatiotemporal Transformer for Distributed Cyber-Physical Threat Prediction in Industrial Internet of Vehicles
proposals.motivation: Modern Industrial Internet of Vehicles (IIoV) systems, integrating autonomous driving and Industry 5.0 principles, face sophisticated cyber-physical threats that manifest as anomalies in spatiotemporal traffic and network data. Pre-2026, centralized threat detection requires pooling sensitive operational data from private industrial clouds, creating severe privacy, security, and regulatory challenges that hinder collaborative defense.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a federated learning framework, built upon a temporal-semantic transformer architecture, can effectively learn a global threat prediction model from distributed, non-IID spatiotemporal IIoV data across private clouds without raw data exchange, matching the accuracy of centralized training.
proposals.method: We will design a Federated Spatiotemporal Transformer Network (Fed-TSTNet), extending the TSTNet architecture for anomaly detection. The core employs a transformer encoder to capture temporal semantics and a graph neural network for spatial vehicle-to-infrastructure dependencies. We will implement a federated averaging (FedAvg) algorithm, enhanced with differential privacy and secure aggregation, to coordinate training across multiple industrial cloud sites. The model will be validated using real-world urban traffic datasets (e.g., PeMS) partitioned to simulate private industrial clouds.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, privacy-preserving framework for collaborative threat intelligence in cyber-physical transportation systems. It will advance the application of federated learning to spatiotemporal anomaly detection, providing a concrete methodology for secure, distributed model training that aligns with Industry 5.0's human-centric and resilient ideals.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the fusion of a temporal-semantic transformer (TSTNet) designed for IIoV with a rigorous federated learning protocol, specifically tailored for the non-IID, correlated spatiotemporal data streams of distributed industrial traffic control systems, a challenge not fully addressed in pre-2026 federated learning or IIoV security literature.

proposals.title: ETP-WE-Lite: A Lightweight Edge Task Processing Framework for Industrial IoT with Workload Estimation
proposals.motivation: The proliferation of Industrial IoT (IIoT) demands real-time, low-latency processing at the network edge, but resource-constrained edge nodes struggle with complex computational tasks. Existing frameworks like ETP-WE (Edge Task Processing with Workload Estimation) are designed for more capable fog/cloud layers and are too heavy for pervasive, low-power edge devices in smart factories. Adapting such frameworks is critical for enabling true Industry 4.0/5.0 applications like predictive maintenance and real-time quality control directly on the shop floor.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by strategically simplifying the spatiotemporal workload estimation model and task scheduling logic of the ETP-WE framework, we can create a functionally effective yet significantly lighter version (ETP-WE-Lite) that operates within the strict memory and compute limits of IIoT edge nodes without critically compromising task completion rates.
proposals.method: We will develop ETP-WE-Lite by first conducting a component-wise complexity analysis of the original ETP-WE framework. The core adaptation involves replacing its dense neural network-based workload estimator with a lightweight Temporal Semantic Transformer (TST) encoder, inspired by TSTNet but with drastically reduced layers and attention heads. A simplified greedy knapsack-based scheduler will replace the more complex optimization solver. The framework will be prototyped in Python and evaluated on a Raspberry Pi 4 cluster simulating an IIoT edge, using synthetic industrial sensor data streams.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a formally specified and open-source lightweight edge processing framework tailored for IIoT. It will provide empirical benchmarks on the trade-offs between model complexity, resource usage (RAM/CPU), and task scheduling performance (throughput, delay) in a constrained edge setting, a gap in current literature.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the systematic 'scale-down' methodology for an existing edge computing framework, specifically targeting the unique latency and resource constraints of industrial edges, rather than simply porting cloud-native solutions. It uniquely combines principles from lightweight transformer design and industrial control systems.

proposals.title: A Spatiotemporal-Aware Virtual Machine Placement Algorithm for Multi-Cloud and Federated Cloud Management
proposals.motivation: The increasing adoption of multi-cloud and federated cloud strategies, driven by needs for resilience, regulatory compliance, and cost optimization, introduces significant challenges in workload management. Pre-2026 research on Virtual Machine Placement (VMP) primarily focuses on single data centers, failing to account for the dynamic, geographically dispersed nature of multi-cloud environments where network latency, data gravity, and provider-specific constraints create complex, time-varying system states. Efficiently orchestrating workloads across such heterogeneous, distributed systems is a critical unsolved problem for future cloud-native applications and services.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a VMP algorithm extended with spatiotemporal modeling and semantic constraints, inspired by principles from urban traffic flow and networked control systems, can significantly optimize global cost, latency, and reliability metrics for workloads distributed across federated cloud providers.
proposals.method: We will extend a baseline VMP algorithm (e.g., a constraint satisfaction or meta-heuristic approach) by integrating a Temporal Semantic Transformer (TSTNet-like) module to model and predict the dynamic state of the multi-cloud network, including compute resource prices, cross-provider bandwidth delays, and regional load. This model will inform a decentralized control framework, treating each cloud provider as an autonomous agent within a larger system, coordinating placement decisions to minimize a global objective function that balances cost, performance (inspired by traffic delay minimization), and data locality constraints, potentially leveraging blockchain for immutable agreement on SLAs.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, spatiotemporally-aware VMP algorithm specifically designed for the federated cloud paradigm. It will provide a formal model and a practical framework for managing workloads across administrative boundaries, directly addressing the scalability and real-time decision-making challenges of multi-cloud systems. The integration of semantic understanding of workload requirements and dynamic system states will advance the field beyond static bin-packing solutions.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the cross-domain application of spatiotemporal anomaly detection and semantic transformer models from intelligent transportation systems (IoV) to the cloud resource management problem, creating a unified view of geographically dispersed compute and network resources as a dynamic, controllable system akin to a traffic network.

proposals.title: Byzantine-Resilient Federated Learning for Intelligent Transportation Systems: A Trust-Aware Spatiotemporal Aggregation Framework
proposals.motivation: Federated learning (FL) is crucial for privacy-preserving model training in distributed systems like the Internet of Vehicles (IoV). However, in real-world deployments, edge nodes (e.g., vehicles, RSUs) can be malicious or unreliable due to cyber-attacks, sensor faults, or data poisoning, acting as Byzantine participants. This compromises the global model's integrity, posing severe risks to traffic flow prediction, autonomous driving decisions, and system control, as seen in prior work on urban traffic anomalies and IoV networks.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating a trust-aware, spatiotemporal aggregation mechanism into cluster-based FL will effectively mitigate the impact of Byzantine edge nodes, leading to more robust and accurate global models for time-sensitive ITS applications compared to standard robust aggregation rules.
proposals.method: We propose a two-layer defense architecture. First, a dynamic trust scoring mechanism will be established for each edge node, evaluating historical model update consistency and local data quality via lightweight anomaly detection inspired by UTFP-AD. Second, we will design a novel Temporal Semantic Aggregation (TSA) protocol, extending concepts from TSTNet, where a primary server performs robust aggregation (e.g., using Multi-Krum or coordinate-wise median) within semantically similar spatiotemporal clusters before a final global fusion. This leverages the inherent correlations in traffic systems to isolate malicious influences.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, trust-aware FL framework specifically designed for the spatiotemporal data and reliability challenges of ITS. It will provide a concrete methodology for integrating Byzantine robustness into cluster-based FL, advancing secure distributed learning for autonomous systems. The evaluation will demonstrate improved model resilience and accuracy under attack scenarios using real-world traffic datasets.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synergistic integration of dynamic trust mechanisms with spatiotemporal semantic clustering for Byzantine resilience, moving beyond generic robust aggregation to exploit the domain-specific structure of IoV systems, which is not addressed by existing standalone FL defense or traffic modeling papers.

proposals.title: EcoEdgeNet: A Spatiotemporal Transformer for Energy-Aware Anomaly Detection in Resource-Constrained Smart Home Systems
proposals.motivation: The proliferation of IoT in smart homes generates vast spatiotemporal data streams, but deploying accurate, real-time anomaly detection models is hindered by limited edge device resources (energy, compute). Pre-2026 research, including the researcher's own work on traffic anomaly detection, prioritizes accuracy, often at unsustainable computational costs for continuous, long-term deployment in residential settings.
