proposals.hypothesis: By explicitly inverting the primary optimization goal from accuracy to energy efficiency and latency, and employing a novel spatiotemporal transformer architecture with adaptive complexity, we can maintain acceptable detection performance while drastically reducing the operational resource footprint on edge devices.
proposals.method: We will adapt the TSTNet/Transformer architecture from the researcher's prior work for smart home sensor data. The core innovation is a multi-objective loss function that directly penalizes energy consumption and inference latency, estimated via a lightweight profiler, alongside a reconstruction error for anomaly detection. We will integrate an adaptive attention mechanism that prunes spatial and temporal heads based on real-time resource constraints. The model will be trained and evaluated on public pre-2026 smart home datasets (e.g., CASAS).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal framework and a practical model for efficiency-first anomaly detection, extending the researcher's expertise in spatiotemporal systems to the sustainable IoT domain. It will deliver concrete metrics on the accuracy-efficiency trade-off frontier, guiding the design of future resource-aware smart home systems.
proposals.novelty: The approach fundamentally shifts the optimization paradigm for edge AI in smart homes, making efficiency the primary constraint rather than a secondary consideration. It novelly combines a transformer-based anomaly detector with a runtime resource profiler within the training loop to directly shape model architecture and inference behavior.

proposals.title: ST-DCR: A Spatiotemporal Dynamic Cluster Reconfiguration Framework for Real-Time Internet of Vehicles Management
proposals.motivation: In large-scale Internet of Vehicles (IoV) and urban traffic systems, static clustering of vehicles or network nodes becomes inefficient as mobility patterns, communication demands, and data distributions shift dynamically. Pre-2026 research, including Dr. Vasilakos's work on traffic flow anomaly detection and temporal transformers for IoV, highlights the challenge of managing real-time delays and system control under such non-stationary conditions. An adaptive clustering mechanism is crucial for maintaining low-latency communication, efficient resource allocation, and robust anomaly detection in evolving urban environments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a spatiotemporal clustering framework, which dynamically reconfigures clusters based on real-time vehicle trajectory data and network load, will significantly reduce average end-to-end delay and improve anomaly detection accuracy compared to static or periodically updated clustering methods.
proposals.method: We propose ST-DCR, integrating a Temporal Semantic Transformer (TSTNet-like) encoder to model evolving vehicle behavior and a graph-based spatiotemporal clustering core. The method will use a dual-threshold mechanism: a local metric (e.g., link stability, semantic similarity) triggers candidate cluster splits/merges, while a global controller, informed by predicted traffic flow patterns (UTFP-AD principles), orchestrates system-wide reconfiguration to optimize a compound objective of latency, load balance, and cluster coherence. Lightweight blockchain-anchored logs (inspired by prior healthcare work) will ensure integrity of reconfiguration decisions for auditability.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel online clustering algorithm for non-stationary spatiotemporal data streams, 2) a validated control framework for dynamic resource partitioning in IoV, and 3) empirical benchmarks showing improvements in communication latency and system adaptability for real-time vehicular applications.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the closed-loop integration of semantic trajectory forecasting with real-time graph clustering, moving beyond periodic updates to event-driven, predictive reconfiguration specifically tailored for the control challenges in IoV systems.

proposals.title: A Federated Learning Architecture for Robust Smart Home Systems: Validation on Diverse, Real-World Non-IID Datasets
proposals.motivation: Smart home systems increasingly rely on distributed machine learning for tasks like activity recognition and energy management. However, existing architectures are often validated on limited, idealized (IID) datasets, failing to capture the significant statistical heterogeneity (non-IIDness) in device usage, user behavior, and environmental contexts found in real-world deployments. This gap leads to models that perform poorly in practice, undermining system reliability and user trust.
proposals.hypothesis: A federated learning architecture incorporating temporal-semantic modeling and adaptive client selection will demonstrate significantly improved robustness and generalization when validated against a diverse corpus of real-world, non-IID smart home datasets, compared to standard federated averaging (FedAvg).
proposals.method: We will design a temporal-semantic transformer-based federated learning architecture, inspired by TSTNet, to model complex spatiotemporal dependencies in heterogeneous device data streams. The architecture will integrate an adaptive client selection mechanism, informed by real-time system state and data distribution metrics, to manage stragglers and non-IID challenges. Validation will be performed on a newly curated benchmark comprising multiple public smart home datasets (e.g., CASAS, ARAS) and simulated deployment scenarios, explicitly measuring performance under varying degrees of data skew and concept drift.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorously validated federated architecture for practical smart home intelligence, moving beyond synthetic IID benchmarks. It will produce a public benchmark of diverse non-IID smart home data scenarios for future research and offer concrete design principles for handling real-world heterogeneity in distributed IoT learning systems.
proposals.novelty: While federated learning for IoT is studied, a dedicated architecture validated across a comprehensive suite of real-world, non-IID smart home datasets is lacking. The novel integration of temporal-semantic transformers (from vehicular networks) with adaptive client selection specifically for the smart home domain's unique heterogeneity patterns is a key innovation.

proposals.title: Optimizing Spatiotemporal Federated Learning for IoV Traffic Forecasting: A Granularity-Efficiency Trade-off Analysis
proposals.motivation: Federated learning (FL) is crucial for privacy-preserving, real-time traffic forecasting in the Internet of Vehicles (IoV), but its efficiency is challenged by system heterogeneity and communication delays. The granularity of client clustering—balancing many fine-grained clusters against fewer, larger ones—directly impacts model performance, convergence speed, and network overhead, yet this trade-off remains unquantified for spatiotemporal IoV data. A systematic analysis is needed to guide efficient FL system design for urban traffic management.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an optimal intermediate cluster granularity exists that maximizes forecasting accuracy while minimizing communication rounds and latency, and that this optimum is dependent on the spatiotemporal correlation patterns of the urban traffic data.
proposals.method: We will extend a Temporal Semantic Transformer (TSTNet) backbone within a clustered FL framework. Using real-world traffic datasets (e.g., PeMS), we will simulate an IoV environment and vary cluster numbers via algorithms like K-Means or spectral clustering based on vehicle mobility patterns. For each configuration, we will rigorously measure test RMSE for flow prediction, total communication cost (bytes transmitted), and training convergence rounds, employing control variables for local epochs and participant fraction.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, data-driven methodology for configuring clustered FL systems in IoV, directly addressing the real-time delay challenges. It will yield a set of actionable guidelines for balancing forecasting precision with system efficiency, advancing the practical deployment of federated intelligence for smart urban traffic systems.
proposals.novelty: While clustered FL is known, the explicit, quantitative analysis of the granularity-efficiency trade-off specifically for spatiotemporal transformer models in IoV traffic forecasting is novel. This bridges the gap between abstract FL theory and the concrete system constraints of real-time vehicular networks.

proposals.title: RL-Split: A Reinforcement Learning Framework for Dynamic DNN Splitting in Vehicular Edge-Cloud Systems
proposals.motivation: The efficient deployment of complex Deep Neural Networks (DNNs) for autonomous driving in the Internet of Vehicles (IoV) is constrained by the trade-off between computational latency and accuracy. Static model splitting between edge and cloud is suboptimal under dynamic network conditions, vehicle mobility, and fluctuating computing resource availability, leading to unpredictable delays and degraded real-time performance. A dynamic, state-aware split mechanism is critical for meeting the stringent Quality of Service (QoS) requirements of safety-critical IoV applications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) agent, trained on a comprehensive system state encompassing network bandwidth, edge server load, and task characteristics, can learn an optimal, adaptive policy for DNN split point selection that minimizes end-to-end latency while preserving inference accuracy.
proposals.method: We will formulate the split point selection as a Markov Decision Process (MDP). The state space will include real-time metrics (e.g., RTT, available edge computing power, vehicle location) and task metadata (e.g., DNN layer dimensions). The action space will be the set of feasible split points in a target DNN (e.g., YOLO, ResNet). We will employ a Deep Q-Network (DQN) or Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) algorithm, training the agent in a simulated IoV environment built on frameworks like SUMO and NS-3. The reward function will penalize latency and accuracy loss.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, closed-loop control framework for adaptive DNN partitioning in IoV, moving beyond static or heuristic-based approaches. It will provide a methodology to formally optimize the latency-accuracy trade-off under dynamic system states, directly enhancing the feasibility of complex AI services in resource-constrained vehicular networks.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the application of model-free DRL to the *intra-model* split point selection problem, integrating a multi-dimensional, temporal system state for holistic control, which is distinct from prior work on static partitioning or RL for task offloading decisions alone.

proposals.title: Cross-Domain Spatiotemporal Transfer Learning for Cold-Start Traffic Anomaly Detection in Urban Clusters
proposals.motivation: Urban traffic management systems often face the cold-start problem when deploying anomaly detection models in new city clusters or regions with sparse historical data, leading to poor initial performance and delays in achieving reliable real-time control. This is a critical challenge for scalable Intelligent Transportation Systems (ITS) as seen in pre-2026 IoV and smart city deployments. Existing methods like UTFP-AD require substantial local data, which is not always available for new deployments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a spatiotemporal transformer model, pre-trained on traffic flow data from established urban domains, can be effectively transferred to bootstrap accurate anomaly detection in cold-start clusters with minimal local fine-tuning, significantly reducing the required initialization data.
proposals.method: We will develop a Temporal Semantic Transformer (TSTNet) based architecture as the core transferable model. The model will be pre-trained on a large-scale, multi-city traffic dataset (e.g., PeMS) for spatiotemporal pattern reconstruction and anomaly detection tasks. For a target cold-start cluster, we will employ a hybrid transfer strategy: first, domain adaptation using adversarial training on limited target data to align feature distributions, followed by few-shot fine-tuning of specific attention heads. We will rigorously evaluate against baselines using only target data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework for rapid deployment of traffic anomaly detection in data-sparse environments, directly addressing a key scalability bottleneck in urban ITS. It will contribute a new benchmark and methodology for cross-domain transfer learning in spatiotemporal traffic systems, extending the principles of TSTNet to the cold-start problem.
proposals.novelty: While transfer learning is established, its application to cold-start *clusters* in traffic anomaly detection, specifically using a transformer architecture adapted via adversarial and few-shot techniques for spatiotemporal data, is novel. This integrates Vasilakos's expertise in traffic systems, temporal semantics, and scalable control into a unified transfer learning paradigm.

proposals.title: HomeFL: A Hierarchical Federated Learning Framework for Resource-Aware and Private IoT Model Training in a Single Smart Home
proposals.motivation: Smart homes contain numerous heterogeneous IoT devices (sensors, appliances, cameras) generating rich, private data streams. Centralizing this data for machine learning models violates user privacy and incurs significant communication overhead. Existing Federated Learning (FL) frameworks are designed for cross-device or cross-silo scenarios across many users, but they are inefficient and lack fine-grained control for the unique constraints and dynamic topology of a single, multi-device domestic environment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a hierarchical, cluster-based FL framework, explicitly designed for the single-home scale, can significantly improve training efficiency and model personalization by intelligently managing device heterogeneity, network delays, and computational resource constraints, while preserving data locality and privacy.
proposals.method: We propose HomeFL, a two-tier FL architecture. At the lower tier, devices within a home are dynamically clustered based on computational capability, data modality, and network connectivity using a lightweight spectral clustering variant. Each cluster elects a local aggregator (e.g., a home gateway or a capable device). We adapt the Temporal Semantic Transformer (TSTNet) concept from the researcher's prior work to model and predict device availability and system delays, enabling proactive scheduling of local training rounds. A lightweight blockchain-inspired ledger, as explored in the researcher's healthcare work, will be used for immutable logging of participation and model updates to ensure integrity and auditability within the home.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel FL system architecture optimized for the single-home, multi-device scale, 2) a resource-aware, delay-sensitive client selection and clustering algorithm, and 3) an empirical evaluation demonstrating improved convergence speed, resource efficiency, and model accuracy for domestic IoT tasks (e.g., activity recognition, energy forecasting) compared to standard FL baselines.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in scaling down and re-engineering the cluster-based FL paradigm for the intra-home environment, integrating temporal delay prediction for real-time scheduling, and applying lightweight integrity mechanisms from blockchain—concepts typically used in large-scale systems—to address the unique challenges of a single, constrained, and privacy-sensitive domain.

proposals.title: Federated Spatiotemporal Learning for City-Wide Urban Sensing: A Scalable Architecture Integrating Smart Homes and Vehicular Networks
proposals.motivation: Current smart city sensing paradigms, such as those for traffic anomaly detection (UTFP-AD) or IoV computing (TSTNet), often rely on infrastructure-based or vehicular sensors, creating coverage gaps and scalability challenges. Federating thousands of smart home IoT devices (e.g., smart meters, environmental sensors) offers an unprecedented, dense sensing fabric for urban analytics, but introduces severe system challenges in real-time data integrity, scalable coordination, and privacy-preserving model training.
proposals.hypothesis: A hierarchical federated learning architecture, secured with lightweight blockchain mechanisms for model integrity, can efficiently integrate heterogeneous data from massive smart home networks and vehicular systems to enable accurate, city-wide spatiotemporal models (e.g., for traffic flow, air quality, energy demand) without centralizing raw data.
proposals.method: We propose a two-tier federated learning system where smart homes act as first-tier clients, training local models on temporal sensor data. Local model updates are aggregated by district-level aggregators (e.g., edge servers), which form the second-tier clients in a wider federation that also incorporates data from vehicular networks (TSTNet). We will adapt a Temporal Semantic Transformer to handle asynchronous, heterogeneous updates and employ a scalable, permissioned blockchain (inspired by prior healthcare work) for transparent, tamper-proof recording of aggregation events and access control. The system's scalability will be evaluated using city-scale simulations with real-world mobility and IoT data traces.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, scalable architectural blueprint for privacy-aware, city-scale federated sensing. It will advance the field by providing a concrete method to unify smart home and vehicular data ecosystems for holistic urban analytics, demonstrated through a case study on joint traffic and environmental anomaly prediction. The integration of blockchain for integrity in a large-scale FL setting is a key systems contribution.
proposals.novelty: While federated learning and blockchain have been explored separately, their integration for scalable, multi-source (home+vehicle) urban sensing is novel. The proposed hierarchical FL architecture specifically addresses the system and control challenges of coordinating thousands of heterogeneous, delay-prone devices, moving beyond single-domain applications like traffic or healthcare.

proposals.title: Generating Privacy-Preserving Smart Home Traffic for System Testing via Inverse Spatiotemporal Generative Models
proposals.motivation: The development and validation of smart home and IoT control systems require extensive, realistic traffic datasets, which are scarce due to privacy concerns and collection costs. Existing synthetic data generation methods often fail to capture the complex spatiotemporal dependencies and multi-modal patterns (e.g., device interactions, user routines) found in real smart environments, limiting the reliability of system testing and benchmarking.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a trained cluster of spatiotemporal models, which accurately captures distinct behavioral patterns in smart home traffic, can be inverted to generate high-fidelity, privacy-preserving synthetic datasets that retain the statistical and temporal properties of the original data while preventing the re-identification of individuals.
proposals.method: We will first apply clustering (e.g., DBSCAN) on real smart home IoT traffic features to identify distinct behavioral patterns. For each cluster, a Temporal Semantic Transformer (TSTNet) model will be trained to learn the underlying spatiotemporal dynamics. The inverse synthesis will be performed by sampling from the latent space of these trained models, using techniques like Variational Autoencoders (VAEs) or Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) conditioned on cluster semantics, to generate novel but realistic traffic sequences. Differential privacy will be integrated into the sampling process to provide formal privacy guarantees.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework for generating semantically rich, privacy-compliant smart home datasets, enabling more robust testing of IoT security, network management, and automation systems. It will also extend the 'inverse problem' paradigm from physical systems to cyber-physical system traffic modeling, offering a new tool for data sharing in sensitive domains.
proposals.novelty: The approach uniquely combines clustering, temporal semantic transformers, and inverse generative modeling specifically for IoT traffic synthesis, moving beyond simple anonymization or rule-based generation to create data that preserves complex systemic behaviors derived from learned models.

proposals.title: Dual-Objective Resource Optimization for Target Accuracy in Vehicular Edge Learning Systems
proposals.motivation: The deployment of real-time AI models for autonomous driving and urban traffic management in the Internet of Vehicles (IoV) is constrained by stringent latency and limited edge/vehicle resources. Current federated or distributed learning approaches often treat accuracy and resource consumption as separate objectives, leading to inefficient systems that either waste energy and bandwidth or fail to meet minimum reliability thresholds for safety-critical tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by formulating a dual-objective optimization problem with a target accuracy constraint, we can derive a dynamic resource allocation strategy that minimizes the total system cost (computation + communication) for vehicular edge learning while guaranteeing a pre-specified model performance level.
proposals.method: We will develop a spatiotemporal optimization framework leveraging a constrained Markov Decision Process (CMDP) model. The core will integrate a lightweight Temporal Semantic Transformer (TSTNet) for predicting local model utility and a primal-dual learning algorithm to solve the resource minimization problem under a probabilistic target accuracy constraint. The system will dynamically adjust the participation of vehicles, local computation cycles, and communication frequency based on real-time network conditions and data distribution, informed by pre-2026 techniques like adaptive federated averaging and network calculus.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal optimization framework and practical algorithms for resource-accuracy trade-offs in IoV. It will contribute a novel CMDP solver tailored for spatiotemporal systems and demonstrate significant reductions in total resource consumption compared to static or accuracy-maximizing baselines in simulated urban traffic scenarios.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the hard constraint formulation (minimize resources *subject to* a target accuracy) rather than a weighted sum, and the integration of semantic-aware transformers within the CMDP to capture the temporal value of vehicular data, moving beyond simple resource-aware federated learning.

proposals.title: FedHome: A Spatiotemporal Cluster Federated Learning Framework for Cross-Domain Smart Home Service Optimization
proposals.motivation: Smart homes generate heterogeneous, privacy-sensitive data streams for services like security, energy management, and healthcare. Current approaches often train isolated models per service, leading to inefficiency, data silos, and an inability to capture cross-domain spatiotemporal dependencies (e.g., occupancy patterns affecting both energy and security). Federated Learning (FL) addresses privacy but standard FL struggles with the non-IID, multi-modal data and diverse latency requirements across these services.
proposals.hypothesis: A hierarchical, cluster-based FL framework that groups homes by spatiotemporal activity patterns can jointly learn a set of specialized yet correlated models for security, energy, and healthcare services, improving overall accuracy, convergence speed, and resource efficiency compared to independent or naive FL training.
proposals.method: We propose a two-tier architecture inspired by Vasilakos's work on spatiotemporal systems. First, a lightweight Temporal Semantic Transformer (TSTNet variant) on edge gateways will extract local spatiotemporal features from multi-service data. Second, a central server will cluster homes based on these feature similarities using an algorithm like DBSCAN. Federated averaging will then be performed within each cluster, but with a novel multi-task learning head that shares a common feature encoder across services while fine-tuning separate output layers for security, energy, and healthcare tasks, balancing shared knowledge and service-specific needs.
proposals.contribution: 1) A novel FL framework for multi-service smart environments that improves model performance and training efficiency through spatiotemporal clustering. 2) A practical solution for cross-domain learning that preserves data privacy and handles non-IID data. 3) Empirical validation on real-world datasets, providing a blueprint for scalable, integrated smart home intelligence.
proposals.novelty: The integration of spatiotemporal clustering within FL specifically for cross-domain service optimization in smart homes is unexplored. It uniquely combines Vasilakos's expertise in traffic pattern analysis (UTFP-AD) and semantic transformers (TSTNet) with FL to solve the multi-service coordination problem, moving beyond single-service FL applications.

proposals.title: Task-Aware Adaptive Encoding for Quantum Machine Learning in Dynamic Vehicular Systems
proposals.motivation: Quantum Machine Learning (QML) offers potential for complex optimization in dynamic systems like urban traffic and autonomous vehicle networks. However, a key bottleneck is the fixed, problem-agnostic quantum encoding (data embedding) strategy, which fails to adapt to the specific algorithmic task and real-time system state, leading to suboptimal circuit expressivity and trainability. This is critical for time-sensitive IoV applications where computational efficiency and model accuracy are paramount.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a spatiotemporal, task-aware adaptive encoding framework, which dynamically adjusts feature mapping based on the target QML algorithm and real-time vehicular data patterns, will significantly enhance model performance, convergence speed, and resource efficiency compared to static encodings.
proposals.method: We will develop a meta-learning controller, inspired by Temporal Semantic Transformer architectures, to analyze the target QML task (e.g., anomaly detection, route optimization) and incoming spatiotemporal traffic data streams. This controller will parameterize and select from a repertoire of encoding circuits (e.g., angle, amplitude, IQP-style embeddings). The selection will be optimized via a lightweight classical neural network, trained with reinforcement learning to minimize a combined metric of quantum resource cost and task loss, integrated within a variational quantum algorithm loop.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, adaptive encoding paradigm for QML, moving beyond one-size-fits-all embeddings. It will provide a concrete software framework for dynamic encoding in variational algorithms and demonstrate quantifiable performance gains in real-world IoV simulation scenarios, such as traffic flow prediction and computing power scheduling.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the closed-loop, task-driven adaptation of the quantum feature map itself, a layer typically held fixed, by leveraging classical meta-learning and spatiotemporal context—a synthesis not explored in pre-2026 QML for cyber-physical systems.

proposals.title: Validating a Spatiotemporal Routing Framework for VLEO Satellite Networks Using Real Ephemeris and Urban Traffic Data
proposals.motivation: The emergence of Very Low Earth Orbit (VLEO) satellite constellations promises ultra-low latency communication for global Internet of Vehicles (IoV) and autonomous driving systems. However, existing routing models often rely on simplified orbital mechanics and synthetic traffic, failing to capture the dynamic spatiotemporal constraints and real-world data patterns critical for reliable, low-delay performance. A validation gap exists between theoretical frameworks and operational reality.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a spatiotemporal routing framework, when validated with real satellite ephemeris data and empirical urban traffic flow patterns, will reveal significant and predictable performance deviations (e.g., in latency and packet loss) compared to simulations using idealized models, enabling the design of more robust and adaptive control algorithms.
proposals.method: We will integrate real two-line element (TLE) ephemeris data for operational VLEO constellations (e.g., Starlink) into a discrete-event network simulator. Real-world urban traffic flow data, pre-processed for anomaly detection using methods akin to UTFP-AD, will generate communication demand patterns. The core routing logic will be based on a Temporal Semantic Transformer architecture (extending TSTNet principles) to model dynamic network states and compute optimal paths. Performance will be benchmarked against a baseline using circular orbital approximations and synthetic traffic.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first publicly documented performance benchmark of a semantic-aware routing framework under real orbital and terrestrial mobility conditions for VLEO-IoV. It will yield a validated simulation testbed and specific insights into how spatiotemporal dynamics impact Quality of Service, directly informing the design of next-generation network control systems for autonomous vehicles.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the concurrent integration of real, dynamic orbital mechanics with real, anomalous urban traffic patterns to stress-test an advanced transformer-based routing model, moving beyond the common practice of evaluating such frameworks in isolated or synthetically simplified environments.

proposals.title: A Spatiotemporal Transformer Framework for Dynamic Tasking and Urgent Data Relay in Satellite-Aerial-Ground Integrated Networks
proposals.motivation: Current remote sensing and data relay systems, such as those for disaster response or military reconnaissance, rely heavily on pre-scheduled missions, leading to critical delays for urgent, unscheduled tasks. The integration of satellite, aerial (UAV), and ground vehicle networks (IoV) offers a solution, but lacks a real-time, adaptive routing and resource orchestration framework capable of handling the dynamic spatiotemporal constraints and heterogeneous communication delays inherent in such complex systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a hierarchical, transformer-based spatiotemporal learning model, integrated with a blockchain-secured control layer, can dynamically optimize multi-hop routing and computing resource allocation for unscheduled tasks, minimizing end-to-end data delivery latency while ensuring system integrity.
proposals.method: We will extend the TSTNet architecture to model the entire satellite-aerial-ground network as a dynamic spatiotemporal graph. A Temporal Semantic Transformer will encode real-time node states (position, velocity, data queue, link quality) and urgent task requests. A hierarchical reinforcement learning (HRL) controller, inspired by urban traffic control methods, will make high-level routing decisions and low-level resource adjustments. Data integrity and access control for task provenance will be managed via a lightweight blockchain layer, as explored in prior healthcare work, adapted for low-latency constraints.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel integrated routing and resource orchestration framework for dynamic missions in heterogeneous networks, 2) a publicly available simulation testbed modeling realistic delays and mobility, and 3) quantitative benchmarks showing latency reduction and reliability improvement over static and heuristic dynamic schedulers.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the fusion of a temporal semantic transformer for state representation with a hierarchical RL controller for decision-making, specifically tailored for the unscheduled task problem, and the co-design of low-latency routing with blockchain-based security for task auditability in a delay-sensitive environment.

proposals.title: Energy-Aware Multiagent Routing for Satellite Networks: Integrating Power Constraints and OISL Costs via Spatiotemporal Deep Reinforcement Learning
proposals.motivation: The proliferation of Low Earth Orbit (LEO) mega-constellations for Internet of Vehicles (IoV) and global sensing introduces critical energy management challenges. Satellite onboard power is finite, and establishing Optical Inter-Satellite Links (OISLs) for data relay is energy-intensive, directly impacting network longevity and service reliability. Existing multiagent routing protocols for satellite networks, however, primarily optimize for latency and throughput, neglecting these physical-layer energy constraints, which risks premature node failure and service disruption.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that formulating the multiagent satellite routing problem with an objective function that explicitly models individual satellite power budgets and heterogeneous OISL energy costs will yield routing policies that significantly extend network operational lifetime and maintain Quality of Service (QoS), compared to latency-only optimization.
proposals.method: We will develop a Spatiotemporal Graph Attention Network (ST-GAT) to model the dynamic satellite topology and traffic demand. A Multiagent Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (MADDPG) framework will be employed, where each satellite is an agent. The critic's reward function will integrate a novel energy cost term, penalizing actions based on the transmitting satellite's remaining power and the specific OISL energy expenditure, alongside traditional delay metrics. Training will use simulated LEO constellations (e.g., Starlink-like) with realistic power consumption models for communication subsystems.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel energy-aware formulation for satellite network routing, 2) a practical MADDPG-based algorithm that learns cooperative, energy-efficient routing policies, and 3) an open-source simulation framework integrating satellite power dynamics with network-level performance evaluation, advancing the field of sustainable space-terrestrial integrated networks.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the joint optimization of communication delays and physical energy consumption at the OISL level within a multiagent deep RL framework, moving beyond abstract link costs to incorporate hardware-specific power constraints—a synthesis not yet addressed in prior satellite networking or IoV research.

proposals.title: FED-SAT: A Federated Learning Framework for Distributed Satellite Network Traffic Control
proposals.motivation: Emerging mega-constellations of Low Earth Orbit (LEO) satellites create a complex, dynamic network for global data relay, where centralized traffic management introduces significant latency and exposes sensitive routing data. Pre-2026 research in terrestrial networks, like the Internet of Vehicles, highlights the need for decentralized, real-time control to manage delays and system challenges. Applying these principles to space-based networks is critical for robust, low-latency communications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a federated learning (FL) framework can train effective, localized routing agents on individual satellites using their observed traffic patterns, and that aggregating these local models will yield a global control policy superior to static or centralized methods while preserving data privacy.
proposals.method: We propose a hierarchical FL architecture where each satellite acts as a client, training a local recurrent neural network (RNN) or lightweight transformer model on its spatiotemporal traffic flow data. A designated orbital-plane aggregator will periodically perform secure model aggregation using the FedAvg algorithm. The framework will integrate concepts from TSTNet for temporal semantic modeling and adapt UTFP-AD principles for on-satellite anomaly detection to trigger local model retraining.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel FL protocol specifically designed for the high-delay, intermittent connectivity constraints of satellite networks. It will provide a validated simulation demonstrating reduced end-to-end latency and improved resilience compared to centralized baselines. The research will also contribute a privacy-preserving paradigm for distributed control in critical infrastructure.
proposals.novelty: While FL is established for terrestrial IoT, its application to in-orbit satellite networks for real-time traffic control is unexplored. The novelty lies in co-designing the FL process with orbital mechanics and intermittent link schedules, moving beyond a direct translation of ground-based methods.

proposals.title: Spatio-Temporal Transformer for Intra-Satellite Data Flow Optimization in Low Earth Orbit Constellations
proposals.motivation: The proliferation of Low Earth Orbit (LEO) satellite constellations (e.g., Starlink, OneWeb) creates a complex, dynamic network where latency-critical tasks like Earth observation analytics and autonomous vehicle command relay are hindered by multi-hop inter-satellite routing. This introduces significant delays and processing bottlenecks. Optimizing data flow and computation within a single satellite node is crucial to minimize these relay dependencies and meet stringent Quality-of-Service requirements for real-time applications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by modeling a single satellite as a dynamic spatiotemporal system and applying advanced on-board flow control, we can significantly reduce end-to-end latency for priority tasks by minimizing the need for inter-satellite relay hops.
proposals.method: We will adapt the Temporal Semantic Transformer (TSTNet) architecture, previously used for vehicular networks, to model the satellite's internal data processing pipeline and queueing dynamics as a temporal graph. A hybrid control policy, combining a Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (DDPG) agent for real-time flow scheduling with a rule-based module for critical traffic (inspired by UTFP-AD anomaly triggers), will be designed. The system will be simulated using a modified OMNeT++ framework integrating real satellite telemetry patterns and traffic loads from pre-2026 LEO studies.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, semantically-aware framework for intra-satellite resource management, directly extending terrestrial IoT and vehicular network control theories to space-based systems. It will provide concrete latency and throughput benchmarks for single-node optimization, offering a foundational model for 'smart satellite' design in future integrated space-air-ground networks.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the conceptual 'scale-down' from network-level to node-level optimization in satellite constellations and the specific adaptation of a Temporal Semantic Transformer—a tool for ground-based traffic—to the unique resource and delay constraints of an orbital computing platform.

ideas.title: Incorporate reinforcement learning for split point selection

ideas.title: Dynamic Recurrent Policy Networks for Robust Control in Stochastic Hybrid Systems with Delays
ideas.description: This research proposes a novel deep reinforcement learning framework for control in stochastic hybrid systems with delays, where the static policy network is replaced by a dynamic recurrent neural network (e.g., a Gated Recurrent Unit or Liquid Time-Constant network). This allows the agent to maintain an internal state that actively models the temporal dependencies and latent mode transitions, effectively learning to filter noisy observations and anticipate the impact of past actions delayed in time. Consequently, the agent does not just output a robust action for a perceived state but dynamically adapts its own representation and decision-making process in response to the evolving system dynamics, leading to policies with superior generalization across unseen delay patterns and jumping mode distributions.

ideas.title: Event-Triggered Control for Resource-Aware Multi-Robot Systems with Delayed Neural Network Coordination
ideas.description: This research fuses event-triggered control with advanced robotic systems to develop a novel control paradigm for complex, networked robotic teams, such as humanoid collectives or heterogeneous swarms. It extends delayed neural network stability analysis to model and manage the intricate, nonlinear interactions and communication delays within these systems. The core innovation is an event-triggered communication protocol that drastically reduces network traffic by transmitting coordination signals only when necessary for stability, rather than continuously. This enables the practical deployment of sophisticated, brain-inspired coordination in resource-constrained real-world scenarios, achieving a balance between performance, robustness, and operational feasibility previously unattained by either idea alone.

ideas.title: Lyapunov-Shielded Deep Reinforcement Learning for Provably Robust Control Under Stochastic Jumps and Delays
ideas.description: This approach combines the exploratory power of deep reinforcement learning with formal guarantees by using a two-layer architecture. The DRL agent learns a performant policy in the complex stochastic jump-delay environment. A parallel module learns or verifies a Lyapunov function candidate for the system dynamics. A runtime shield monitors the agent's proposed actions and the system state, permitting only those actions that satisfy the Lyapunov stability condition, ensuring all trajectories remain within a provably stable region. This results in a policy that is both high-performing and certifiably robust.

ideas.title: Dual: minimize resource use for target accuracy

ideas.title: Stability Analysis of Recurrent Neural Networks with Unconstrained Time-Varying Delays
ideas.description: This research direction investigates the stability and convergence properties of recurrent neural networks (RNNs) or other dynamic neural architectures where the internal or inter-neuron communication is subject to time-varying delays that are not required to be differentiable or slowly varying. The core challenge is to develop novel Lyapunov-Krasovskii functionals or inequality techniques that can handle the combined complexity of the network's inherent dynamics and the unconstrained delay variations. The outcome would be a robust theoretical framework for certifying the safety and reliability of RNNs deployed in real-time systems with unpredictable lag, such as networked control or distributed AI. This directly addresses a critical gap in applying dynamic neural models to latency-sensitive, real-world environments.

ideas.title: Asynchronous Self-Triggered Control for Multi-Agent Flexible Robotic Networks under Unmatched Premise Variables
ideas.description: This research develops a novel self-triggered coordination scheme for networks of collaborative, flexible-joint robots. It specifically removes the conventional requirement for matched or constrained-mismatched premise variables across agents, allowing each robot to operate with fully asynchronous and independent scheduling variables based on its local state and estimates. This enables resilient collaboration in dynamic tasks despite heterogeneous hardware, variable communication latencies, and unsynchronized local event triggers. The resulting framework guarantees stability and performance for the multi-agent system while drastically reducing unnecessary communication and computation compared to traditional time-triggered or synchronous event-triggered approaches.

ideas.title: Integrate real-time weather and event data

ideas.title: Reinforcement Learning for Self-Triggered Control of Soft Robots under Stochastic Delays
ideas.description: This research fuses stochastic delay modeling for soft robots with reinforcement learning (RL) for rule tuning to create an adaptive, self-triggered control framework. The core idea is to use an RL agent to dynamically tune not only the fuzzy rules but also the parameters of the stochastic event-triggering condition itself, based on observed delay distributions and the soft manipulator's continuously changing dynamics. This enables the soft robot to autonomously learn an optimal control-and-communication policy that minimizes energy use and wear while robustly completing tasks in unpredictable environments. The synergy lies in the co-adaptation of the triggering mechanism and the control policy, specifically tailored to the unique challenges of soft robotic systems.

ideas.title: Robust Adaptive Model Predictive Control for Triggering with Online Uncertainty Quantification
ideas.description: This approach enhances the standard MPC-for-triggering framework by directly addressing its fragility to model inaccuracies. It combines an online adaptive element that learns and corrects the system model from streaming data with a robust MPC optimization that plans triggering instants against the worst-case realization within a dynamically updated uncertainty set. This ensures the triggering policy remains safe and performance-aware even under significant model drift or disturbances. The result is a self-correcting, robust event-triggered control system that maintains reliability in unpredictable environments.

ideas.title: Safe Reinforcement Learning with Control Barrier Functions and a Digital Twin for Stable Fuzzy Rule Tuning
ideas.description: This redesigned system applies reinforcement learning (RL) for fuzzy rule tuning within a rigorously safe framework. A real-time safety shield, based on control barrier functions, continuously filters the RL agent's actions to ensure all system constraints and stability criteria are met. A pre-optimized nominal policy provides a stable baseline, while a synchronized high-fidelity digital twin allows for risk-free exploration and pre-evaluation of rule adjustments. This approach guarantees safe online learning, preventing catastrophic failures during exploration and accelerating convergence to an improved, robust rule set.

ideas.title: Co-Design of Plant Dynamics and Self-Triggered Policy for Maximum Robustness to Delay
ideas.description: This research direction proposes a unified co-design framework that jointly optimizes physical system parameters (e.g., mechanical compliance, actuator bandwidth, sensor placement) and the parameters of a self-triggered communication policy. The objective is to maximize the tolerable time delay or uncertainty margin while guaranteeing stability and performance. This creates a synergistic loop: the plant can be 'softened' or tuned to create longer safe intervals for communication, while the trigger policy is adapted to exploit the newly engineered temporal robustness. The result is a cyber-physical system whose hardware and software are intrinsically matched to achieve the greatest possible resilience against delayed or sporadic information updates.

ideas.title: Adaptive Fuzzy Rule Tuning via Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning Inspired by Ecosystem Management
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from Ecological Systems Management, where Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL) is used to dynamically tune intervention policies to maintain ecosystem stability based on delayed, stochastic feedback. The analogous solution is applied to the problem of tuning fuzzy rules in a self-triggered controller. The adapted research idea proposes a framework where each fuzzy rule or a cluster of related rules is modeled as an autonomous reinforcement learning agent. These agents operate within a cooperative MARL setting, receiving a shared reward signal based on global control performance metrics (e.g., tracking error, settling time) and the efficiency of the self-triggering mechanism (e.g., reduction in actuation frequency). Through exploration and experience, the agents learn to dynamically adjust their associated rule parameters—such as the shapes of input/output membership functions, rule weights, or consequent actions—to collaboratively optimize the overall control policy. This enables the fuzzy rule base to self-evolve and adapt to changing system dynamics or operational goals, maintaining control performance while minimizing communication and computation triggers.

ideas.title: Output-Feedback Self-Triggered Control with Online Markovian Delay Estimation
ideas.description: This research direction investigates the co-design of a self-triggered output-feedback controller and an online estimator for Markovian network-induced delays. The core challenge is to jointly estimate the system's latent state and the active delay mode from only intermittent, noisy output samples, which are themselves triggered based on the current delay and state estimates. The resulting scheme eliminates the need for both continuous full-state sensing and a priori knowledge of the delay dynamics, making it applicable to sensor networks and remote control over unreliable channels. The key innovation is the closed-loop interdependence where the triggering condition actively probes the system to improve delay mode identification, while the refined delay estimate enhances the precision of future triggering and control actions.

ideas.title: Resilient Self-Triggered Control of Multi-Link Flexible Robots under Network-Induced Uncertainties
ideas.description: This research develops and validates a novel resilient self-triggered control scheme for multi-link flexible robotic manipulators that explicitly accounts for data packet loss in the feedback network. The core challenge is to design a unified stability analysis and triggering rule that jointly manages the energy propagation across multiple flexible links and the information gaps caused by network unreliability. The work will produce a control law that actively compensates for lost sensor data by leveraging the robot's distributed flexibility model, leading to a co-design of communication scheduling and vibration damping. Experimental validation on a complex multi-link setup will demonstrate robustness to network degradation while maintaining performance and reducing overall communication and actuation effort.

ideas.title: Adaptive MPC for Event-Triggered Control using Ecological Foraging Strategies
ideas.description: This idea employs a model predictive control (MPC) framework to predict and schedule future triggering instants by drawing an analogy from optimal foraging theory in behavioral ecology. In ecology, animals solve the analogous problem of deciding when to trigger a move to a new foraging patch by using internal models to predict resource depletion and travel costs, optimizing their long-term energy intake rate. The adapted solution is to design an MPC-based triggering mechanism where the controller maintains a predictive model of both the plant dynamics and a 'resource landscape' analogous to information utility or control performance. The triggering decision is formulated as a predictive patch-leaving rule: an event is triggered (i.e., a control update is communicated) when the MPC's rolling-horizon optimization determines that the predicted marginal cost of maintaining the current triggering policy (e.g., accumulating state uncertainty or performance degradation) outweighs the predicted cost of triggering an update (e.g., communication bandwidth, energy). This transforms the triggering problem into a predictive, adaptive resource allocation problem, optimizing the trade-off between control performance and communication or computation resource use over a future horizon.

ideas.title: Self-Triggered Fault-Tolerant Control for Stochastic Jump Systems with Actuator Saturation and Hybrid Faults
ideas.description: This research synthesizes a novel control architecture for stochastic jump systems (e.g., networked systems with random component failures or parameter changes) that explicitly models actuator saturation and a spectrum of fault modes (partial loss, bias, locking). It integrates a self-triggered mechanism with a unified fault detection, isolation, and tolerance scheme. The key innovation is the closed-loop interdependence: the fault diagnosis informs the self-triggering rule to adjust its conservatism, while the adaptive triggering instants provide refined data for fault estimation, all while synthesizing control signals that proactively avoid saturation under the newly identified fault-induced constraints. This results in a resilient system that optimally balances communication/computation resource usage with guaranteed performance and stability in the presence of stochastic jumps, actuator limits, and evolving faults.

ideas.title: Inverse Reinforcement Learning for Interpretable and Optimal Self-Triggered Fuzzy Control
ideas.description: This research direction proposes using inverse optimal control to identify a meaningful cost function that explains the stability and performance of a baseline self-triggered fuzzy controller under stochastic delays. This identified cost function then serves as the reward signal for a reinforcement learning agent tasked with dynamically tuning the controller's fuzzy rules. The synergy produces a self-improving control system where the RL's exploration is guided by a mathematically grounded objective, guaranteeing that the adaptive tuning converges toward policies that are optimal for a cost function inherently linked to the system's stable behavior, merging data-driven adaptation with analytical guarantees.

ideas.title: Deep Meta-RL for Control with Stochastic Jumps and Delays, Inspired by Portfolio Optimization
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from quantitative finance, where deep RL is used to manage portfolios in regime-switching markets with execution delays—a problem analogous to control under stochastic jumping modes and delays. The analogous solution uses a POMDP framework where the agent maintains a belief state over hidden market regimes. We adapt this by formulating the control problem as a POMDP, where the system's active dynamic mode and delay are partially observable. We propose to train a deep reinforcement learning agent (e.g., using a recurrent or meta-RL architecture) to maintain a belief state from a history of observations and actions. The agent will learn a control policy that is robust to the stochastic jumps and delays by optimally acting based on its inferred belief about the current hidden system state, directly transferring the core methodology from the financial domain to robust control engineering.

ideas.title: Energy-Aware Self-Triggered Control with Dynamic Saturation Management for Flexible Joint Robots
ideas.description: This research proposes a novel self-triggered fuzzy control framework that dynamically manages actuator saturation by explicitly accounting for the energy stored in the joint flexibility. The core innovation is a supervisory layer that estimates the elastic energy state and predicts its evolution under saturated control inputs. This prediction directly informs both the computation of a safe, energy-dissipating control action and the adaptive scheduling of the next control update, ensuring stability is formally guaranteed even during prolonged saturation. The approach moves beyond static constraint handling to provide a co-designed control and triggering law that is robust to the nonlinear interactions between saturation limits and flexible dynamics.

ideas.title: Adaptive robust control for Markov jump systems with non-stationary, learnable delay distributions
ideas.description: This research develops a framework for systems with mode-dependent delays where the probability distributions governing both the Markov chain transitions and the delay values are not just unknown but potentially time-varying. The core innovation is a dual-layer adaptive mechanism: a higher-level meta-model tracks the evolution of the underlying stochastic parameters, while a lower-level robust controller guarantees stability under the current distributional estimates. The approach integrates online data-driven distribution learning with constrained robust control design, ensuring provable performance despite the non-stationarity. The result is a system that actively reduces its own uncertainty over time while maintaining safety and stability envelopes.

ideas.title: Composite Learning-Based Adaptive Control with Delay-Consistent Data Selection for Robotic Systems
ideas.description: This research develops a composite learning adaptive control framework for robots with parametric uncertainty and stochastic network-induced delays. The system guarantees parameter convergence without persistent excitation by constructing a history stack from past, delay-compensated input-output data. A robust predictor and a consistency-checking module actively select only temporally valid data for the adaptive update law, preventing instability caused by delayed or out-of-sequence feedback. This results in a globally stable controller that achieves accurate parameter identification and robust tracking performance even under intermittent, variable-time delays.

ideas.title: Inferring Network Latency Jumps from Application Performance Traces
ideas.description: This idea applies the core principle of network tomography—inferring hidden internal network states from end-point observations—to the dual problem of delay estimation. Instead of controlling a system with a known delay model, the goal is to estimate the dynamics of the Markovian jumping delays themselves by observing the system's state. The analogous solution from internet measurement uses statistical inverse techniques on sequential performance data to map observations to latent network conditions. Adapted here, this involves formulating the delay estimation as an inverse problem where the observed state sequence is the input, and the output is the most likely sequence of delay modes, potentially using tools like hidden Markov model filters or Bayesian inference to perform 'delay tomography' and uncover the latent delay process affecting the control loop.

ideas.title: Real-Time, Fault-Adaptive Self-Triggered Control via On-the-Fly LMI Synthesis
ideas.description: This research develops a unified framework where efficient, real-time LMI solvers are not just used to verify pre-computed stability conditions, but to actively synthesize new self-triggered control laws on-the-fly. Upon fault detection, the system immediately formulates a new, fault-accommodating LMI problem with reduced computational complexity, leveraging the optimized solver to find a stabilizing controller and a new, resource-aware event-triggering rule before the system's state diverges. This creates a closed loop where fault tolerance directly and dynamically co-designs the control law and the communication/sampling strategy, achieving a level of resilient agility impossible with offline fault-tolerant design or with efficient solvers used only for nominal verification.

ideas.title: Implement control law on physical hardware using a Hardware-in-the-Loop (HIL) validation pyramid
ideas.description: This idea adapts the structured Hardware-in-the-Loop (HIL) validation pyramid from aerospace flight control development to the problem of transitioning a theoretical LMI-based control law to real-time implementation on flexible joint robot hardware. In aerospace, systems are de-risked by progressing from software simulation to HIL testing with real control hardware interacting with a simulated plant, and finally to full physical rig tests. Adapted to robotics, the solution is to create a multi-stage validation protocol: first, a high-fidelity software simulation of the flexible joint dynamics; second, HIL testing where the LMI control law executes on the target robot's real-time processor (e.g., a dedicated microcontroller or FPGA) while receiving sensor feedback from and sending commands to a real-time simulation of the plant; and finally, gradual physical integration beginning with a single flexible joint on a test bench. This approach systematically exposes and resolves implementation challenges like real-time computational bottlenecks, quantization effects, sensor noise filtering, and actuator bandwidth limitations before full deployment on the complex, multi-joint physical system.

ideas.title: Adversarially Robust Co-Design of Triggering and Control Under Bounded Delay Attacks
ideas.description: This research investigates the joint optimization of a dynamic event-triggering policy and a control law to maintain stability and performance when an adversary can maliciously delay transmitted data packets up to a known maximum bound. The problem is formulated as a two-player zero-sum game or a minimax optimization, where the control/triggering agent minimizes a system cost (like an L2-gain) while the delay-inducing adversary maximizes it. The solution seeks a Nash equilibrium policy pair, providing a guaranteed worst-case performance bound for any delay sequence within the adversary's capability. This yields a resilient co-designed system that is provably robust to strategic delay attacks, moving beyond passive analysis to active defense.

ideas.title: Dynamic Neural Networks via Ecological Succession Principles
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption of a static neural network structure by drawing an analogy from ecology. In ecology, models evolved from static snapshots to dynamic succession frameworks, where an ecosystem's species composition and interaction network change over time in response to internal state and environmental pressures. Adapting this solution, we propose neural networks where the architectural components—connectivity, depth, and neuron types—are not fixed but undergo a form of 'computational succession.' The network's structure dynamically reorganizes based on feedback from the data stream (the 'environment') and its own internal activity state. This results in a model whose functional architecture is a temporally emergent property, potentially allowing for more accurate, efficient, and adaptive modeling of complex, non-stationary processes.

ideas.title: Stability-Constrained Meta-Gating for Input-Adaptive Dynamic Networks
ideas.description: This system replaces static networks with a dynamic architecture governed by a lightweight, meta-learned gating controller. The controller evaluates input samples and selectively activates only necessary computational pathways, making the network efficient and adaptive. A stability loss term penalizes erratic architectural fluctuations, ensuring training convergence and reliable inference. Finally, an architecture replay mechanism preserves performance on previously seen task domains, preventing catastrophic forgetting during the network's evolution.

ideas.title: Robust Stability for Multi-Agent Flexible-Joint Robots under Dynamic Topology and Delays
ideas.description: This research develops a stability analysis and control synthesis framework for networks of cooperative flexible-joint robots that must operate reliably despite dynamic changes in inter-agent communication links and persistent delays. The approach models the system as a switched nonlinear system where discrete events correspond to changes in the communication graph topology, such as link failures or agent dropouts. Stability is guaranteed by constructing a family of Lyapunov-Krasovskii functionals and establishing dwell-time constraints for the switching signals, ensuring the team's cooperative task performance is maintained under realistic network conditions. The resulting control laws are validated in simulation for scenarios involving intermittent connectivity.

ideas.title: Robust Self-Triggered Control under Co-Occurring Unknown Delay Distributions and Stochastic Packet Loss
ideas.description: This research investigates the stability of self-triggered control systems where the feedback loop is closed over a network subject to both stochastic packet loss and mode-dependent, random delays. Crucially, the joint probability distributions governing the delay modes and the loss process are considered partially unknown or belong to an uncertainty set. The core challenge is to co-design the self-triggering threshold and the control law to ensure robust stability against this combined, imperfectly modeled network effect. This leads to a novel analysis using tools from robust Markov jump linear systems and chance-constrained optimization to provide probabilistic stability guarantees under realistic network uncertainty.

ideas.title: Adaptive Control with Dual-Estimation for Robotic Systems with Parameter Uncertainty and Stochastic Delays
ideas.description: This research expands the original seed idea by drawing an analogy from power systems engineering, where adaptive control handles uncertain renewable generation inputs alongside stochastic communication delays in grid sensors. The analogous solution employs a dual-adaptive architecture combining parameter estimation with dedicated delay compensation. Adapted to robotics, the proposed method develops a unified adaptive control framework that concurrently estimates uncertain robot parameters (like inertia and friction) and compensates for stochastic network or computational delays. This is achieved by integrating a real-time parameter adaptation law with a parallel delay predictor/estimator, fusing their outputs to compute a robust control signal that ensures stability and performance despite the dual challenges of model uncertainty and variable time delays.

ideas.title: Apply Adaptive Parameter Tuning from Computational Neuroscience to Event-Triggered Control
ideas.description: This idea draws an analogy from computational neuroscience, specifically from models of synaptic meta-plasticity like the Bienenstock-Cooper-Munro (BCM) theory. In BCM theory, a neuron's synaptic modification threshold adapts based on its recent firing history to stabilize learning and optimize information coding. Analogously, in event-triggered adaptive control, the parameters governing the self-triggering condition (e.g., thresholds, evaluation intervals) are typically set offline. The adapted solution is to implement a meta-learning layer that continuously tunes these triggering parameters online. This layer would monitor the recent history of control performance, disturbance profiles, and the resulting event frequency, then adjust the triggering condition's sensitivity and timing to dynamically optimize the balance between resource usage (e.g., network bandwidth, processor cycles) and control fidelity, leading to a more robust and resource-efficient adaptive control system.

ideas.title: Distributed Stability Control for Resource-Constrained Micro-Robot Swarms with Flexible Actuation
ideas.description: This research investigates the stability of cooperative multi-agent systems where the agents are micro-scale robots with flexible joint actuators. The core challenge is to develop a distributed control and communication framework that rigorously guarantees swarm stability despite the combination of inherent actuator flexibility, inter-agent communication delays, and the severe onboard resource constraints (e.g., limited processing, memory, and energy) of each micro-agent. The analysis must explicitly model how these constraints force simplified, event-triggered, or quantized communication strategies, which in turn interact with the flexible dynamics and delays to create new closed-loop stability conditions. The outcome will be a co-design principle linking micro-agent hardware limitations to provably stable swarm behaviors.

ideas.title: Identify the Sparse, Predictive Cost Function from a Stable Controller under Stochastic Delays
ideas.description: This research addresses the non-uniqueness of the classical inverse optimal control problem by framing it as a constrained identification task. Given a stable controller and a stochastic delay model, the system searches the space of feasible cost functions for the one that is both maximally sparse/interpretable and generalizable. The approach employs regularization for sparsity and cross-validation across different delay statistics to ensure the recovered cost function is the unique, predictive explanation for the observed control policy, moving beyond mere feasibility to functional identifiability.

ideas.title: Co-design of predictive compensation and robust self-triggered control under bounded packet loss
ideas.description: This research develops a self-triggered control framework that is robust to network packet loss by co-designing the control law with an active compensation mechanism. A model-based predictor at the controller generates state estimates when packets are lost, and the triggering condition and control update are explicitly designed to be robust to the resulting estimation error. The stability analysis provides formal guarantees on system performance, such as ultimate boundedness or practical stability, under a known maximum allowable packet loss rate. This approach ensures the control system actively mitigates the impact of data loss rather than merely analyzing its potential effects.

ideas.title: Adaptive Self-Triggered Control via Biological Homeostasis Mechanisms
ideas.description: This research adapts principles from biological homeostasis to relax the known delay bound assumption in self-triggered control. In systems biology, organisms maintain stability despite unknown, variable environmental disturbances through adaptive feedback mechanisms like allostasis and hormesis—not fixed thresholds. The analogous solution involves sensor-driven adaptation rates, predictive anticipation, and dynamic sensitivity adjustment. Adapted to networked control systems, this leads to a scheme where the triggering condition is no longer based on a pre-defined maximum delay. Instead, the controller employs a moving window to estimate recent delay patterns and network congestion, dynamically adjusting the triggering threshold via a lightweight online learning rule (inspired by hormonal regulation dynamics). This creates a self-triggered mechanism that maintains stability and performance without prior knowledge of the delay bound, responding adaptively to actual, time-varying network conditions.

ideas.title: Delay-Compensated Hybrid Dynamic Event-Triggered Control with Zeno-Free Guarantees for Neural Network Systems
ideas.description: This research designs a robust event-triggered control framework specifically for delayed neural network systems that guarantees avoidance of Zeno behavior and maintains stability under disturbances. It combines a hybrid dynamic event-triggering mechanism (DETM) with a model-based predictor to actively compensate for network delays, decoupling the communication reduction logic from the delay-induced performance loss. A key feature is the formal enforcement of a strictly positive minimum inter-event time, ensuring the control strategy is physically implementable. The approach provides rigorous analytical guarantees for ultimate boundedness or asymptotic stability, validated through numerical simulations on benchmark neural network control problems.

ideas.title: Dual: Use genomics to improve LLM training

ideas.title: Meta-Controlled Hierarchical Integration of Self-Triggered Control and Fault Diagnosis for Guaranteed Performance and Reliability
ideas.description: This research designs a robust control architecture that guarantees both resource efficiency and fault tolerance without performance trade-offs. It employs a hierarchical structure with a self-triggered nominal controller, a separately scheduled fault diagnosis observer, and a supervisory meta-controller for arbitration. The meta-controller uses formal rules to seamlessly switch between a resource-saving nominal mode and a fault-tolerant control law only when necessary, preventing false alarms from disrupting system efficiency. This ensures reliable fault accommodation is achieved while rigorously preserving the communication and computation benefits of the self-triggered approach during normal operation.

ideas.title: Test on larger, more diverse smart grid datasets

ideas.title: Stability-Condition-Consistent Online Estimation for Self-Triggered Switched Systems with Markovian Delays
ideas.description: This research fuses switched system theory with online Markovian delay estimation to create a novel co-design methodology for self-triggered control. The core idea is to model the self-triggered system with network-induced delays as a delay-dependent switched system, where the switching signal is influenced by both the triggering rule and the unknown, time-varying delay mode. An online estimator concurrently infers the active delay mode from state observations, but its structure and confidence bounds are explicitly shaped by the Lyapunov-based stability conditions derived for the switched system. This bidirectional interaction ensures the online estimates are 'stability-aware,' allowing the controller to adapt its triggering parameters safely and leading to less conservative, data-informed stability guarantees compared to designs based on a priori worst-case delay assumptions.

ideas.title: Inverse Optimal Control via Inverse Statistical Mechanics
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from statistical mechanics to solve the inverse optimal control problem. In statistical mechanics, one infers an energy function (Hamiltonian) from an observed equilibrium distribution. Analogously, given a stable controller (an observed policy), we aim to find the cost function for which it is optimal under a stochastic delay model. The adapted solution treats the controller's behavior as a probabilistic policy arising from an optimal control problem with an unknown cost. By applying a maximum entropy principle or a likelihood-based inference framework, we can formulate and solve an optimization problem to recover the cost function parameters that best explain the given controller, effectively performing 'inverse reinforcement learning' specifically tailored for systems with stochastic delays.

ideas.title: Optimize LMI computation for faster solving

ideas.title: Adversarial Hardware-in-the-Loop Robustness Validation for Multi-Link Flexible Robot Control
ideas.description: This research designs a rigorous validation framework that moves beyond simple demonstration to stress-test proposed control schemes for multi-link flexible manipulators against realistic failure modes. A Hardware-in-the-Loop (HIL) system is implemented, where the physical controller hardware and software interact in real-time with a high-fidelity, computationally efficient dynamic model of the complex flexible multi-body system. The core of the approach is an adversarial testing protocol that systematically injects disturbances, model mismatches, sensor noise, and actuator constraints to probe the boundaries of stability and performance. The outcome is a controller that is not merely validated but robustly certified against a defined operational design domain, significantly de-risking subsequent physical implementation.

ideas.title: Self-triggered control via output-based event generation using observer-based prediction
ideas.description: This research expands the original idea by drawing an analogy from medical diagnostics, where clinicians use partial vital signs to estimate unmeasured internal states and trigger interventions based on those estimates. The analogous solution involves using physiological models to reconstruct full patient state from sparse measurements and predicting when critical thresholds will be crossed. Adapted to control systems, the idea is to implement a self-triggered control scheme without full state feedback by employing a state observer or estimator that reconstructs the state from output measurements. The triggering mechanism is then designed using the observer's state estimate and its predicted evolution: the next control update time is computed based on when a predicted output-based error (or a function of the estimated state) is expected to exceed a designed threshold. This removes the need for full state measurement while retaining the benefits of self-triggered control in reducing communication and computation burden.

ideas.title: Self-triggered control with stability-guaranteed fallback for unknown time-varying delays
ideas.description: This design enhances robustness by integrating a self-triggered predictor for unknown delays with a stability-monitoring supervisor. The supervisor continuously evaluates a real-time stability metric derived from available system data. If the metric indicates potential instability due to delay misestimation, the system automatically switches to a guaranteed-safe, high-frequency periodic sampling mode to regain stability and recalibrate. This hybrid approach ensures formal stability guarantees while maintaining the communication efficiency benefits of self-triggered control during normal operation.

ideas.title: Exploiting Sparsity and Structure in LMI Computation via Analogies to Quantum Chemistry
ideas.description: This research idea proposes to develop more efficient algorithms for solving Linear Matrix Inequality (LMI) stability conditions by drawing an analogy to computational quantum chemistry. In quantum chemistry, solving the Schrödinger equation for large molecules was made tractable by algorithms that exploit sparsity, hierarchical structure, and approximations of long-range interactions (e.g., the Fast Multipole Method). We adapt this solution to the control context. The goal is to create novel LMI solvers that do not treat the problem as dense but instead analyze the underlying control system's structure—such as the connectivity in large-scale networked systems or the decay of influence in spatially distributed systems—to identify sparse and hierarchical patterns within the LMI. The adapted algorithm would use this structure to deploy focused computational effort, employing tailored pre-conditioners and iterative schemes that approximate weaker couplings while solving strong interactions precisely. This structural approach aims to break the cubic computational bottleneck of generic semidefinite programming, enabling the real-time implementation of advanced robust and optimal control laws for large-scale, complex systems like power grids or robot swarms.
